option_judgments.justification: Evidence shows electrochemical energy alone does not cause TRP under adiabatic conditions; universalizing this is an unsupported overclaim.

option_judgments.label: Incorrect
option_judgments.justification: Evidence shows the abused cell does not reach decomposition onset under adiabatic conditions; non-adiabatic framing inverts the actual finding.

option_judgments.label: Correct
option_judgments.justification: Evidence states that without tab heat transfer paths, all non-initiation cells decompose simultaneously rather than sequentially.

analysis.target_cognitive_object: Effect of excluding tab connections on TRP prediction mode and timescale validity under adiabatic external-short abuse
analysis.primary_boundary: Adiabatic, no tab connections, LFP 18650 3×3 pack, external short abuse only
disallowed_extrapolations: Generalizing the no-TRP outcome to non-adiabatic conditions as a necessary condition
Claiming sequential decomposition occurs when tabs are excluded

solver_view.stem: A 3×3 LFP 18650 pack is subjected to an instantaneous 250°C temperature rise in a single corner cell. Two scenarios are compared: (i) fully adiabatic boundaries, and (ii) convective and radiative heat loss to a 60°C environment. Which statements correctly characterize the difference in outcomes between these two scenarios?
options: A. Under adiabatic conditions, TRP occurs throughout the pack and the abused cell reaches approximately 675°C average temperature.
B. With external heat loss, TRP is prevented; adjacent cells decompose only ~15% compared to ~40% in the adiabatic case.
C. With external heat loss, TRP still occurs but is delayed relative to the adiabatic case due to slower heat accumulation.
D. Under adiabatic conditions, adjacent cells decompose to a lesser extent than under the heat-loss scenario because convection accelerates reaction.
E. The 250°C instantaneous rise is sufficient to trigger TRP under adiabatic conditions but not under external heat-loss conditions, demonstrating a boundary-condition-dependent outcome.
F. External heat loss to a 60°C environment eliminates all decomposition in non-initiation cells entirely.
G. The adiabatic result cannot be extrapolated to predict pack behavior in ventilated enclosures without accounting for heat loss magnitude.

option_judgments.label: Correct
option_judgments.justification: Evidence directly states TRP occurs throughout the pack under adiabatic conditions, with abused cell reaching ~675°C average.

option_judgments.label: Correct
option_judgments.justification: Evidence states heat loss prevents TRP and limits adjacent cell decomposition to 15% versus ~40% in the adiabatic case.

option_judgments.label: Incorrect
option_judgments.justification: Evidence shows TRP does not occur with heat loss; claiming delayed TRP is an unsupported causal overclaim.

option_judgments.label: Incorrect
option_judgments.justification: Evidence shows adjacent cells decompose more (~40%) under adiabatic conditions, not less; direction is reversed.

option_judgments.label: Correct
option_judgments.justification: Same abuse trigger yields TRP under adiabatic but not under heat-loss conditions, directly demonstrating boundary-condition dependence.

option_judgments.label: Incorrect
option_judgments.justification: Evidence states adjacent cells still decompose 15% with heat loss; complete elimination is an unsupported overclaim.

option_judgments.label: Correct
option_judgments.justification: Adiabatic and heat-loss scenarios yield opposite safety outcomes; extrapolating adiabatic results to ventilated enclosures without heat-loss data is unsupported.

analysis.target_cognitive_object: Boundary-condition dependence of TRP outcome for identical abuse trigger in LFP pack
analysis.primary_boundary: Same 250°C instantaneous abuse; adiabatic vs. convective/radiative loss to 60°C environment
disallowed_extrapolations: Claiming heat loss merely delays rather than prevents TRP
Claiming adiabatic results apply to ventilated real-world packs without qualification

solver_view.stem: An engineer models a 3×3 LFP 18650 pack and must decide whether to include tab connections as heat transfer paths. Evidence from a 2D model shows that omitting tabs changes both the predicted propagation pattern and the safety outcome. Which of the following statements about tab inclusion are NOT supported by the evidence?
options: A. Including tabs can change a model prediction from a safe outcome to an unsafe TRP outcome.
B. Omitting tabs causes non-initiation cells to decompose sequentially, accurately replicating physical pack behavior.
C. Tab connections represent a negligible heat transfer path and their omission does not affect TRP prediction.
D. The timescales for TRP predicted without tabs are likely overestimates relative to a physical pack.
E. Omitting tabs is a conservative modeling choice that always underestimates TRP severity.
F. Tab inclusion is significant enough that it can determine whether a model predicts a safe or unsafe outcome.

option_judgments.label: Correct (supported, so NOT the answer to 'not supported')
option_judgments.justification: Evidence explicitly states tabs can mean the difference between predicting safe vs. unsafe outcomes; this IS supported.

option_judgments.label: Correct (unsupported — answer to stem)
option_judgments.justification: Evidence states omitting tabs causes simultaneous, not sequential, decomposition; sequential is the opposite of what is reported.

option_judgments.label: Correct (unsupported — answer to stem)
option_judgments.justification: Evidence explicitly calls tabs a significant heat transfer path; claiming negligibility directly contradicts the evidence.

option_judgments.label: Correct (supported, so NOT the answer)
option_judgments.justification: Evidence states TRP timescales without tabs are likely large overestimates; this IS supported.

option_judgments.label: Correct (unsupported — answer to stem)
option_judgments.justification: Evidence shows omitting tabs can produce a false-safe prediction, not a conservative overestimate; universal conservatism claim is unsupported.

option_judgments.label: Correct (supported, so NOT the answer)
option_judgments.justification: Evidence directly states tab inclusion can determine safe vs. unsafe model outcome; this IS supported.

analysis.target_cognitive_object: Admissibility of claims about tab connection modeling impact on TRP prediction accuracy and conservatism
analysis.primary_boundary: 2D LFP 18650 3×3 pack model; tab connections as heat transfer paths only
disallowed_extrapolations: Claiming tab omission is always conservative
Claiming sequential decomposition occurs without tabs

solver_view.prompt: A safety engineer uses a 2D thermal model of a 3×3 LFP 18650 pack to assess TRP risk. The model excludes tab connections and uses adiabatic boundary conditions. The model predicts no TRP when a single cell is abused by external short circuit, but predicts TRP when the same cell receives an instantaneous 250°C temperature rise. Identify two distinct modeling limitations that affect the reliability of these predictions, explain the mechanism by which each limitation distorts the result, and state the direction of the resulting prediction error (overestimate or underestimate of TRP risk).

ground_truth.gold_answer: First limitation: exclusion of tab connections. Tabs are a significant heat transfer path between cells. Without them, heat from the initiation cell cannot propagate selectively to neighbors, causing all non-initiation cells to decompose simultaneously rather than sequentially. This also means TRP timescales are likely large overestimates. More critically, omitting tabs can yield a false-safe prediction where the model shows no TRP, whereas including tabs would predict TRP — an underestimate of risk in that scenario. Second limitation: adiabatic boundary conditions. Adiabatic conditions eliminate heat dissipation to the environment. Evidence shows that when realistic convective and radiative losses to a 60°C environment are included, TRP does not occur even under the 250°C instantaneous abuse that causes TRP under adiabatic conditions. Therefore, adiabatic boundaries overestimate TRP risk by preventing any heat from leaving the pack.
must_include: tab connections as a significant heat transfer path
omitting tabs causes simultaneous rather than sequential decomposition
omitting tabs can produce a false-safe (underestimate of risk) prediction
adiabatic conditions remove heat dissipation, overestimating TRP risk
with realistic heat loss, TRP does not occur for the same 250°C abuse trigger
disallowed_extrapolations: Claiming tab omission always overestimates TRP risk
Claiming adiabatic conditions always represent a worst-case for all abuse types
Generalizing LFP outcomes to other chemistries
Claiming the 2D model is otherwise fully representative of a physical pack

analysis.target_cognitive_object: Direction and mechanism of prediction error introduced by two modeling simplifications: tab exclusion and adiabatic boundaries
analysis.primary_boundary: 3×3 LFP 18650 2D model; specific abuse modes (external short vs. instantaneous temperature rise); specific boundary conditions
intended_error_modes: unsupported_monotonicity (assuming tab omission is always conservative)
regime_transfer_error (applying adiabatic conclusions to real ventilated packs)
causal_overclaim (asserting one limitation dominates without evidence)

solver_view.stem: A battery engineer compares two 18650 cells—one with an NCA cathode and one with an LFP cathode—under accelerating rate calorimetry (ARC) at 100% SOC. ARC data show the NCA cell's self-heating rate is four orders of magnitude higher than the LFP cell's. The LFP cathode's exothermic decomposition onset is 310°C. Which statements are CORRECT regarding the relative thermal hazard of these two chemistries?
options: A. The NCA cell poses a substantially higher thermal runaway propagation risk than the LFP cell under equivalent abuse conditions, consistent with its far higher self-heating rate.
B. Because LFP's decomposition onset is 310°C, an LFP cell cannot undergo thermal runaway below that temperature under any abuse scenario.
C. The four-orders-of-magnitude heating rate difference indicates that NCA releases heat far more aggressively once self-heating begins, even if both cells eventually reach runaway.
D. LFP ranks lowest in cathode-electrolyte heat release among LCO, NCA, NCM, LMO, and LFP, consistent with its high decomposition onset and low heating rate.
E. The ARC heating rate difference alone is sufficient to conclude that NCA cells will always ignite adjacent cells in a module while LFP cells will never propagate thermal runaway.
F. Calendar aging affects NCA and LFP cells identically, so aging state does not alter the relative hazard ranking between the two chemistries.

option_judgments.label: Correct
option_judgments.justification: Four-orders-of-magnitude higher heating rate directly supports substantially greater propagation risk for NCA under equivalent abuse.

option_judgments.label: Incorrect
option_judgments.justification: 310°C is the exothermic decomposition onset, not an absolute runaway floor; other reactions (SEI, anode) can initiate runaway at lower temperatures.

option_judgments.label: Correct
option_judgments.justification: Four orders of magnitude difference in heating rate implies far more aggressive heat release for NCA once self-heating initiates, regardless of final runaway state.

option_judgments.label: Correct
option_judgments.justification: Evidence explicitly ranks LFP lowest in cathode-electrolyte heat release and reports its decomposition onset as 310°C, both consistent with lowest hazard.

option_judgments.label: Incorrect
option_judgments.justification: Heating rate difference is a necessary but not sufficient condition; module geometry, gaps, and vent location also govern propagation. Universal claim is unsupported.

option_judgments.label: Incorrect
option_judgments.justification: Evidence describes aging effects on SEI thickness and runaway onset temperature but does not state identical effects across chemistries; universal equivalence is unsupported.

analysis.target_cognitive_object: Relative thermal hazard ranking of NCA vs. LFP using heating rate, heat release rank, and decomposition onset temperature
analysis.primary_boundary: ARC heating rate and decomposition onset apply to cathode-level reactions; module-level propagation requires additional geometric factors
disallowed_extrapolations: LFP decomposition onset as absolute runaway floor
ARC rate alone predicts module-level propagation universally
Aging effects are identical across chemistries

solver_view.stem: A 100% SOC LG 18650 MJ1 cell undergoes thermal runaway in a calorimetry experiment. Total energy released is 74.9 kJ, of which 78.3% is transferred by ejecta and vented gases. A safety engineer proposes that thermal runaway propagation in a 25-cell module is primarily driven by conductive heat transfer through cell casings. Geometric factors tested include cell-to-cell gap, overhead gap, and vent hole location. Which statements correctly characterize the dominant heat transfer pathways and their design implications?
options: A. The 78.3% energy fraction carried by ejecta and gases implies that conduction through cell casings is the dominant propagation pathway, supporting the engineer's proposal.

B. Since ejecta and vented gases carry the majority of released energy, module designs that redirect or contain gas flow are more likely to mitigate propagation than those focused solely on inter-cell thermal insulation.
C. Cell-to-cell gap and overhead gap affect how hot vent gases contact neighboring cells, making geometric configuration a legitimate design variable for propagation control.

D. Convective heat transfer from VOC and H2 combustion accounts for 82% of the preheating process leading to propagation, reinforcing that gas-phase phenomena dominate over conduction in propagation scenarios.
E. Vent hole location on the cell is irrelevant to propagation because total energy released is fixed by electrochemical state regardless of geometry.
F. The 78.3% gas/ejecta energy fraction is a universal constant applicable to all lithium-ion cell formats and chemistries at any SOC.

option_judgments.label: Incorrect
option_judgments.justification: 78.3% carried by ejecta/gases directly contradicts the claim that conduction is dominant; this reverses the reported trend.

option_judgments.label: Correct
option_judgments.justification: Majority energy via gases/ejecta logically implies gas-flow management is more impactful than inter-cell insulation alone for propagation mitigation.

option_judgments.label: Correct
option_judgments.justification: Evidence explicitly states cell-to-cell gap, overhead gap, and vent hole location significantly affect propagation to neighboring cells via hot vent gas flow.

option_judgments.label: Correct
option_judgments.justification: 82% of preheating attributed to convective heat from VOC and H2 combustion directly supports gas-phase dominance over conduction in propagation.

option_judgments.label: Incorrect
option_judgments.justification: Evidence explicitly identifies vent hole location as a significant factor in propagation; dismissing it is contradicted by the module-level findings.

option_judgments.label: Incorrect
option_judgments.justification: The 78.3% figure is reported for one specific cell (LG 18650 MJ1) at 100% SOC; generalizing to all formats and chemistries is an unsupported scale transfer overclaim.

analysis.target_cognitive_object: Dominant heat transfer pathway during thermal runaway and its implications for module-level propagation mitigation design
analysis.primary_boundary: 78.3% gas/ejecta fraction is specific to LG 18650 MJ1 at 100% SOC; 82% convective preheating applies to EV module context studied
disallowed_extrapolations: Conduction dominates propagation
Energy fraction is universal across chemistries/formats/SOC
Vent hole location is irrelevant to propagation

solver_view.stem: A lithium-ion battery module is subjected to an internal short circuit (ISC). An engineer states: 'A small ISC area that self-extinguishes within seconds is equivalent in thermal runaway risk to a large persistent ISC, because both generate resistive heat.' Separately, a calendar-aged cell is tested under ARC and shows thermal runaway onset at a lower temperature than a fresh cell, despite delayed self-heating onset. Which options correctly identify flaws or valid aspects of these two claims?
options: A. The engineer's equivalence claim is flawed: ISC area and persistence duration are both necessary conditions for heat to exceed the threshold that triggers propagating abuse reactions.
B. The engineer's claim is valid because resistive heating rate depends only on current and resistance, not on ISC area or duration.
C. The aged cell's lower runaway onset temperature is consistent with a thicker SEI layer impeding lithium-ion diffusion, which alters the thermal stability profile.
D. Calendar aging always accelerates self-heating onset, so the observation of delayed self-heating in the aged cell contradicts the evidence and should be rejected.
E. A small, brief ISC cannot trigger thermal runaway regardless of cell chemistry or SOC, because the heat generated is always below the abuse reaction threshold.
F. The delayed self-heating onset and lower runaway temperature in the aged cell represent two separable effects of aging on different thermal processes, not a contradiction.

option_judgments.label: Correct
option_judgments.justification: Evidence states ISC must be large AND persist long enough for heat to exceed the threshold triggering propagating abuse reactions; both conditions are necessary.

option_judgments.label: Incorrect
option_judgments.justification: Resistive heating rate depends on current squared times resistance, but whether that heat triggers runaway depends on area and duration—ignoring those is a causal overclaim.

option_judgments.label: Correct
option_judgments.justification: Evidence directly states TR starts at lower temperatures in aged cells due to thicker SEI impeding lithium-ion diffusion, supporting this mechanistic explanation.

option_judgments.label: Incorrect
option_judgments.justification: Evidence states aging DELAYS self-heating onset, not accelerates it; this option reverses the reported trend direction.

option_judgments.label: Incorrect
option_judgments.justification: Evidence does not support a universal lower bound; it states that sufficient area and duration can push heat past the threshold, implying small/brief ISC is conditionally safe, not universally.

option_judgments.label: Correct
option_judgments.justification: Delayed self-heating onset and lower runaway temperature reflect aging effects on different processes (SEI growth affecting diffusion vs. onset kinetics), consistent with evidence.

analysis.target_cognitive_object: ISC area-duration threshold for runaway triggering and separable aging effects on self-heating onset vs. runaway temperature
analysis.primary_boundary: ISC threshold requires both large area AND sufficient duration; aging effects on onset delay and runaway temperature are mechanistically distinct
disallowed_extrapolations: Small ISC universally safe regardless of conditions
Aging always accelerates self-heating onset
Resistive heating rate alone determines runaway risk

solver_view.prompt: A containerized battery energy storage system (BESS) experiences a gas explosion event during thermal runaway. Engineers debate whether top vent panels are necessary, arguing that the container structure alone can absorb overpressure. Separately, the SEI layer on graphite anodes begins decomposing at 80–120°C, generating C2H4, CO2, and O2. Explain (1) what specific hazards arise in the absence of top vent panels, (2) why the gases generated during SEI decomposition are relevant to the explosion hazard in a confined enclosure, and (3) what design principle connects these two observations for BESS safety.

ground_truth.gold_answer: Without top vent panels, the container faces unacceptable overpressure and external hazards including jet flames and high-speed airflow during a gas explosion. This means the structure alone cannot safely absorb explosion loads. SEI decomposition at 80–120°C produces C2H4 (flammable), CO2, and O2; in a confined enclosure, flammable gas accumulation combined with an oxygen source creates conditions for deflagration or explosion when an ignition source is present. The connecting design principle is that venting must be engineered to relieve pressure and direct hazardous gas flows away from personnel and critical structures before ignition thresholds are reached, rather than relying on structural containment alone. Top vent panels serve as a deliberate pressure-relief pathway that reduces peak overpressure and controls jet flame direction.
must_include: unacceptable overpressure without top vent panels
jet flames and high-speed airflow as external hazards
C2H4 as a flammable gas product of SEI decomposition
O2 generation contributing to oxidizing atmosphere in confined space
venting as pressure-relief design principle rather than structural containment
disallowed_extrapolations: Claiming top vent panels eliminate all thermal runaway hazards
Stating SEI decomposition gases alone are sufficient to cause explosion without confinement and ignition conditions
Generalizing container overpressure findings to open-rack or non-containerized BESS formats

analysis.target_cognitive_object: Connection between confined-space gas accumulation from SEI decomposition and the necessity of engineered pressure relief in containerized BESS
analysis.primary_boundary: Overpressure and jet flame findings apply to containerized BESS geometry; SEI gas generation applies to graphite anode cells at 80–120°C
intended_error_modes: causal_overclaim about venting eliminating all hazards
mechanism_substitution conflating gas generation with explosion without confinement condition

solver_view.stem: A 24 Ah NCM111 pouch cell undergoes thermal runaway characterization under both oven and adiabatic (ARC) test protocols. The onset temperature of thermal runaway (T2) measured in the oven test is 48.1°C lower than that measured in the ARC test. An engineer proposes four explanations for this gap. Which of the following statements correctly identify valid mechanistic contributors to this T2 discrepancy?
options: A. In the oven test, external heat flux drives the cell to T2 before internal self-heating reactions alone would have reached the same threshold, effectively lowering the apparent T2.
B. In the ARC test, the adiabatic boundary suppresses heat loss, allowing internal exothermic reactions to accumulate and push T2 to a higher value than under external heating.
C. The oven test measures T2 at the surface, which is the hottest location under external heating, so the recorded T2 is inherently lower than the internal T2 captured in ARC.

D. Under ARC conditions, the jelly roll interior reaches T2 first and at a higher temperature because internal reaction heat is not lost to surroundings, whereas oven heating forces the surface to T2 first.
E. The 48.1°C gap is primarily caused by differences in cell chemistry activation energy between the two test protocols, not by boundary condition differences.

option_judgments.label: Correct
option_judgments.justification: External oven heating supplements internal heat generation, advancing T2 onset below what self-heating alone achieves in ARC.

option_judgments.label: Correct
option_judgments.justification: Adiabatic boundary retains all internally generated heat, allowing reactions to accumulate and elevate T2 relative to oven conditions.

option_judgments.label: Incorrect
option_judgments.justification: Under oven heating the surface is not necessarily the hottest location; C002 shows surface T3 is lower than interior, reversing this logic.

option_judgments.label: Incorrect
option_judgments.justification: Attributes correct ARC mechanism but incorrectly claims oven heating forces surface to T2 first universally; C005 shows trigger sequence depends on oven temperature.

option_judgments.label: Incorrect
option_judgments.justification: No evidence supports chemistry activation energy differences between protocols; the gap is boundary-condition-driven, not chemistry-driven.

analysis.target_cognitive_object: Mechanistic attribution of T2 discrepancy between oven and ARC test protocols
analysis.primary_boundary: Discrepancy is boundary-condition-driven (external heat input vs. adiabatic self-heating accumulation), not chemistry-driven
disallowed_extrapolations: Generalizing oven surface-first trigger to all oven temperatures
Attributing T2 gap to activation energy differences

solver_view.stem: Under adiabatic test conditions, a pouch cell's thermal runaway spatial profile is measured. T3 (peak temperature) at the surface is approximately half that at internal jelly roll positions (JR_a, JR_b) and center. Additionally, the maximum temperature rate at JR_a and JR_b is approximately an order of magnitude higher than at the center and surface. An engineer evaluates the following statements about this spatial profile. Which statements are INCORRECT?
options: A. The lower T3 at the surface compared to internal positions is caused by heat consumed along the transfer path through the jelly roll and shell.
B. The surface temperature rate being lower than at JR_a/JR_b implies that thermal runaway reactions initiate and intensify primarily within the jelly roll under adiabatic conditions.

C. Because T3 at the surface is half that at internal positions, the surface is a safer thermal runaway monitoring point and will always underestimate peak internal temperatures regardless of test protocol.

D. The order-of-magnitude difference in temperature rate between JR nodes and the center/surface indicates that the center and surface experience similar reaction intensities to JR_a and JR_b but with delayed timing.
E. Under adiabatic conditions, increasing battery thickness would cause T3 at the surface to increase because more internal heat is generated and must eventually reach the surface.

option_judgments.label: Correct
option_judgments.justification: Directly supported: heat consumed in transfer path through jelly roll and shell reduces surface T3 to approximately half internal values.

option_judgments.label: Correct
option_judgments.justification: Lower surface temperature rate and order-of-magnitude higher JR rates together support jelly roll as the primary reaction locus under adiabatic conditions.

option_judgments.label: Incorrect
option_judgments.justification: Overclaims universality; under oven conditions trigger sequence and spatial T profiles differ, so surface underestimation is not protocol-independent.

option_judgments.label: Incorrect
option_judgments.justification: Order-of-magnitude rate difference indicates fundamentally different reaction intensities, not mere timing delay; center/surface are not equivalent reaction sites.

option_judgments.label: Incorrect
option_judgments.justification: Evidence shows T3 at surface decreases by ~30°C with increasing thickness under adiabatic conditions, opposite to this claim.

analysis.target_cognitive_object: Spatial T3 and temperature rate distribution under adiabatic conditions and their mechanistic interpretation
analysis.primary_boundary: Adiabatic test conditions only; surface underestimation and thickness effects are protocol- and condition-specific
disallowed_extrapolations: Extending adiabatic spatial profile conclusions to oven test
Treating rate difference as timing difference

solver_view.stem: A pouch cell model is used to study oven-test thermal runaway as oven temperature increases from 150°C to 200°C, and separately as battery thickness increases from 1.44 cm to 10 cm at fixed oven temperature. Consider the following statements about T2 behavior and trigger-sequence shifts under these two parametric sweeps. Select all correct statements.
options: A. As oven temperature increases from 150°C to 200°C, T2 at the surface increases while T2 at the center decreases, consistent with a shift in trigger sequence from center-first to surface-first.
B. As oven temperature increases, T2 at both surface and center decrease monotonically because higher external temperature accelerates all internal reactions uniformly.

C. At battery thickness of 10 cm under oven conditions, the T2 difference between JR_a and the center exceeds 20% of the center value, linked to a trigger-sequence reversal from JR_b→JR_a to JR_a→JR_b above 5 cm thickness.
D. Under adiabatic conditions, T2 at all internal positions remains nearly constant as thickness increases from 1.44 cm to 15 cm, because adiabatic boundaries decouple T2 from geometric scaling.

E. The trigger-sequence reversal observed in the oven test above 5 cm thickness also occurs under adiabatic conditions, since jelly roll geometry governs trigger order independent of boundary conditions.
F. Under adiabatic conditions, T3 at the surface decreases by approximately 30°C as thickness increases, because a larger fraction of internal heat is consumed in the longer transfer path.

option_judgments.label: Correct
option_judgments.justification: Directly supported: trigger sequence shifts center→surface to surface→center with rising oven temperature, causing stated T2 directional changes.

option_judgments.label: Incorrect
option_judgments.justification: Reverses the reported trend for surface T2, which increases with oven temperature; uniform monotonic decrease is unsupported.

option_judgments.label: Correct
option_judgments.justification: Supported: >20% T2 difference at 10 cm linked to JR_b→JR_a to JR_a→JR_b trigger reversal above 5 cm under oven conditions.

option_judgments.label: Correct
option_judgments.justification: Supported: under adiabatic conditions T2 remains nearly constant across thickness range 1.44–15 cm at all positions.

option_judgments.label: Incorrect
option_judgments.justification: Extends an oven-test-specific trigger reversal to adiabatic conditions without evidence; boundary conditions alter heat delivery and trigger sequence.

option_judgments.label: Correct
option_judgments.justification: Supported: surface T3 decreases ~30°C with thickness increase under adiabatic conditions due to greater heat consumption in longer transfer path.

analysis.target_cognitive_object: Parametric sensitivity of T2 trigger sequence and T3 to oven temperature and thickness under distinct boundary conditions
analysis.primary_boundary: Oven-test trigger reversal findings are not transferable to adiabatic conditions; T2 thickness insensitivity is adiabatic-specific
disallowed_extrapolations: Applying oven trigger-sequence reversal to adiabatic protocol
Claiming uniform T2 decrease with oven temperature

solver_view.prompt: A battery safety engineer compares two design scenarios for a scaled-up NCM111 pouch cell. In Scenario A, battery thickness is increased from 1.44 cm to 15 cm and tested under adiabatic conditions. In Scenario B, the same thickness increase is tested under oven conditions at a fixed oven temperature. The engineer claims that increasing thickness uniformly worsens thermal runaway severity at all positions and under both protocols. Using mechanistic reasoning, identify at least two specific ways in which the evidence contradicts this uniform-worsening claim, and explain the underlying mechanisms responsible.

ground_truth.gold_answer: The uniform-worsening claim is contradicted in at least two ways. First, under adiabatic conditions, T3 at the surface decreases by approximately 30°C as thickness increases from 1.44 cm to 15 cm. The mechanism is that a thicker cell presents a longer heat transfer path from the jelly roll to the surface; more heat is consumed (dissipated) within the jelly roll and shell along this path, so less thermal energy reaches the surface, reducing peak surface temperature. Second, under adiabatic conditions, T2 at all positions remains nearly constant with increasing thickness, meaning onset severity does not worsen with scale under adiabatic boundaries. Third, under oven conditions, thickness above 5 cm triggers a reversal in the thermal runaway initiation sequence (JR_b→JR_a shifts to JR_a→JR_b), indicating that the spatial progression of runaway changes non-monotonically rather than uniformly worsening everywhere.
must_include: surface T3 decreases under adiabatic conditions with increasing thickness
heat consumed in transfer path through jelly roll and shell as mechanistic explanation
T2 remains nearly constant under adiabatic conditions across thickness range
trigger sequence reversal under oven conditions above 5 cm thickness
disallowed_extrapolations: Claiming surface T3 decrease also occurs under oven conditions without qualification
Asserting T2 thickness insensitivity applies under oven test conditions
Generalizing trigger sequence reversal to adiabatic protocol

analysis.target_cognitive_object: Multi-condition, multi-position evaluation of thickness effects on thermal runaway severity metrics T2 and T3
analysis.primary_boundary: Thickness effects on T2 and T3 are protocol-specific; adiabatic and oven results must not be interchanged

solver_view.stem: An 18650-format lithium-ion battery undergoes thermal runaway. The internal temperature rises from 90°C and continues increasing. Considering the sequential thermal events affecting the SEI film and separator, which of the following statements correctly describe the thermal progression? Select all that apply.
options: A. SEI metastable components begin decomposing at 90–120°C, transforming into stable substances such as Li2CO3.
B. Between 120°C and 250°C, SEI decomposition is complete and no further SEI-related reactions occur.
C. Between 120°C and 250°C, SEI re-decomposition and regeneration reactions occur simultaneously.
D. Separator melting at approximately 130–165°C causes an internal short circuit, after which battery temperature can rapidly exceed 300°C.
E. Separator melting is the trigger for initial SEI decomposition, which then drives temperature above 300°C.
F. Once the separator melts, the battery temperature rise is gradual because regeneration reactions absorb heat.

option_judgments.label: Correct
option_judgments.justification: Evidence states SEI decomposition begins at 90–120°C with metastable components converting to Li2CO3.

option_judgments.label: Incorrect
option_judgments.justification: Evidence explicitly states SEI re-decomposition and regeneration co-occur between 120–250°C, not that reactions cease.

option_judgments.label: Correct
option_judgments.justification: Evidence directly states SEI re-decomposition and regeneration reactions co-occur between 120°C and 250°C.

option_judgments.label: Correct
option_judgments.justification: Evidence states separator melts at 130–165°C causing short circuit, with temperature rapidly rising above 300°C.

option_judgments.label: Incorrect
option_judgments.justification: Causal order is reversed; SEI decomposition initiates at 90–120°C, well before separator melting at 130–165°C.

option_judgments.label: Incorrect
option_judgments.justification: Evidence states temperature rises rapidly above 300°C after separator melting; no gradual rise or net endothermic effect is supported.

analysis.target_cognitive_object: Sequential thermal event thresholds: SEI decomposition onset, SEI co-reaction regime, separator-triggered short circuit and rapid temperature escalation.
analysis.primary_boundary: Temperature-dependent regime transitions: 90–120°C (SEI onset), 120–250°C (co-reaction), 130–165°C (separator melt).
disallowed_extrapolations: Claiming SEI reactions cease before 250°C
Attributing SEI initiation to separator melting
Inferring endothermic net effect from regeneration reactions

solver_view.stem: A battery safety engineer compares 18650-format NMC, NCA, LCO, and LFP cells at identical capacity under thermal runaway. She also evaluates the effect of Ni content within NMC variants (NMC111 vs. NMC811). Which of the following conclusions are supported by experimental evidence? Select all that apply.
options: A. NMC and NCA generate more gas per unit capacity than LCO and LFP under thermal runaway conditions.
B. LFP generates more gas per unit capacity than LCO under thermal runaway conditions.
C. Increasing Ni proportion in NMC chemistry increases both CH4 and C2H4 volume fractions in generated gas.
D. NMC111 produces higher CH4 and C2H4 proportions than NMC811 because lower Ni content stabilizes the cathode.
E. LCO generates more gas per unit capacity than LFP under thermal runaway conditions.
F. NCA produces more gas per unit capacity than NMC under thermal runaway conditions.

option_judgments.label: Correct
option_judgments.justification: NMC (99.33) and NCA (93.73) mmol/Ah exceed LCO (53.14) and LFP (45.45) mmol/Ah per evidence.

option_judgments.label: Incorrect
option_judgments.justification: Evidence shows LFP (45.45 mmol/Ah) generates less gas than LCO (53.14 mmol/Ah), not more.

option_judgments.label: Correct
option_judgments.justification: Evidence states increasing Ni proportion increases CH4 and C2H4 volume fractions, with NMC811 highest.

option_judgments.label: Incorrect
option_judgments.justification: Evidence shows NMC111 has the lowest CH4 (5.88%) and C2H4 (1.57%), opposite to the claim.

option_judgments.label: Correct
option_judgments.justification: LCO (53.14 mmol/Ah) exceeds LFP (45.45 mmol/Ah) per reported gas generation capacity values.

option_judgments.label: Incorrect
option_judgments.justification: Evidence shows NMC (99.33 mmol/Ah) exceeds NCA (93.73 mmol/Ah); NCA does not surpass NMC.

analysis.target_cognitive_object: Rank ordering of gas generation capacity across cathode chemistries and effect of Ni content on hydrocarbon gas fractions in NMC.
analysis.primary_boundary: 18650 format, thermal runaway conditions, per-unit-capacity normalization.
disallowed_extrapolations: Generalizing 18650 rankings to other form factors
Inferring NCA > NMC from proximity of values without evidence

solver_view.stem: A researcher uses an offline gas detection method to study thermal runaway gas emissions from LFP batteries under overcharge and overheating conditions. A colleague suggests the offline method fully characterizes the gas generation process. Which statements correctly identify limitations or mechanistic distinctions relevant to this experimental setup? Select all that apply.
options: A. Offline detection can accurately measure gas type and total concentration but cannot resolve the temporal order of gas generation.
B. Under overcharge conditions, the LFP safety valve opens due to electrolyte vapor pressure, whereas under overheating it opens due to internal gas release.
C. Under overheating conditions, the LFP safety valve opens due to electrolyte vapor, whereas under overcharge it opens due to internal gas release.
D. Gas sensors mounted outside the battery shell detect initial internal gas generation in real time without accumulation delay.
E. H2 is typically the first gas detected during thermal runaway in LFP batteries under both overcharge and overheating conditions.
F. Offline detection methods can capture gas generation rate and temporal evolution of species concentrations.

option_judgments.label: Correct
option_judgments.justification: Evidence states offline methods measure type and concentration accurately but cannot resolve generation order, rate, or time evolution.

option_judgments.label: Incorrect
option_judgments.justification: Causal assignments are swapped; evidence attributes overcharge valve opening to gas release and overheating to electrolyte vapor.

option_judgments.label: Correct
option_judgments.justification: Evidence states overheating opens the valve via electrolyte vapor and overcharge via internal gas release.

option_judgments.label: Incorrect
option_judgments.justification: Evidence states external sensors detect gas only after internal accumulation reaches a threshold; real-time internal detection is not achieved.

option_judgments.label: Correct
option_judgments.justification: Evidence states H2 is the first gas detected under both overcharge and overheating conditions in LFP batteries.

option_judgments.label: Incorrect
option_judgments.justification: Evidence explicitly states offline methods cannot obtain gas generation rate or temporal concentration changes.

analysis.target_cognitive_object: Limitations of offline gas detection and mechanistic distinction between overcharge vs. overheating safety valve triggers in LFP batteries.
analysis.primary_boundary: LFP chemistry; offline vs. online detection capability boundary; overcharge vs. overheating trigger distinction.
disallowed_extrapolations: Generalizing LFP valve-opening mechanism to other chemistries
Claiming external sensors provide real-time internal gas data

solver_view.prompt: An engineer is designing an early-warning gas detection system for lithium-ion battery packs. She is evaluating two strategies: (1) deploying a WO3/Pd sensor tuned for electrolyte vapor detection, and (2) relying solely on H2 sensors mounted outside the battery shell. Using your knowledge of thermal runaway gas generation mechanisms and detection limitations, explain: (a) why H2 is a critical early-warning target despite binder decomposition contributing little heat, and (b) what fundamental limitation affects both external sensor strategies when trying to detect the earliest internal gas generation events.

ground_truth.gold_answer: (a) H2 is the first gas detected during thermal runaway in LFP batteries under both overcharge and overheating conditions, making it a leading indicator of thermal runaway onset. Although the binder decomposition reaction contributes little to the overall heat of thermal runaway, it is the primary pathway for H2 production. Critically, H2 has a lower explosion limit of only 4.0%, meaning even small quantities pose severe fire and explosion risk, justifying prioritizing H2 detection regardless of its limited calorific contribution. (b) Both external sensor strategies share the fundamental limitation that they detect gas only after it has accumulated to a threshold concentration outside the battery shell. The initial gas generation events occurring inside the battery are not captured in real time; the measured signal represents delayed, accumulated gas rather than the true onset of internal generation. This means neither strategy can reliably detect the earliest internal gas generation events.
must_include: H2 is the first gas detected during thermal runaway in LFP batteries
binder decomposition is the primary H2 production pathway
H2 lower explosion limit is 4.0%
external sensors detect gas only after accumulation to a threshold outside the shell
initial internal gas generation is not effectively detected by external methods
disallowed_extrapolations: Claiming binder decomposition contributes significantly to thermal runaway heat
Claiming external sensors can detect real-time internal gas generation onset
Generalizing LFP gas emission order to all battery chemistries without qualification
Claiming WO3/Pd sensor overcomes the accumulation-threshold limitation

analysis.target_cognitive_object: Multi-hop reasoning linking H2 production mechanism (binder decomposition), explosion hazard threshold (4.0% LEL), gas emission sequence, and the fundamental detection limitation of external sensor placement.
analysis.primary_boundary: LFP chemistry for gas emission order; external sensor accumulation-threshold limitation applies to all external detection methods including WO3/Pd.
intended_error_modes: causal_overclaim attributing H2 to cathode decomposition
scale_transfer overclaim assuming external sensor captures internal onset
mechanism_substitution confusing heat contribution with gas production primacy

solver_view.stem: An 18650 NCA cell undergoes thermal abuse. A thermal model tracks sequential failure events as temperature rises. Which of the following event–temperature pairings are consistent with the documented failure sequence for this cell chemistry? Assume all thresholds refer to the same 18650 NCA cell under thermal abuse conditions.
options: A. SEI layer decomposition initiates at approximately 69°C, representing the first exothermic event.
B. Separator shrinkage and internal short circuit occur at approximately 130°C.
C. NCA cathode exothermic oxygen-releasing decomposition begins at approximately 160°C.
D. Separator shrinkage occurs before SEI decomposition, at approximately 90°C.
E. NCA cathode decomposition onset precedes separator shrinkage, occurring near 120°C.
F. Internal short circuit is triggered by electrolyte decomposition at 69°C, not separator failure.

option_judgments.label: Correct
option_judgments.justification: SEI decomposition at ~69°C is the documented first thermal event for 18650 NCA cells.

option_judgments.label: Correct
option_judgments.justification: Separator shrinkage at ~130°C causing internal short circuit matches the documented threshold.

option_judgments.label: Correct
option_judgments.justification: NCA cathode exothermic decomposition onset at ~160°C is explicitly documented for this chemistry.

option_judgments.label: Incorrect
option_judgments.justification: Reverses the documented sequence; SEI decomposition at ~69°C precedes separator shrinkage at ~130°C.

option_judgments.label: Incorrect
option_judgments.justification: Cathode decomposition onset is ~160°C, which follows separator shrinkage at ~130°C, not precedes it.

option_judgments.label: Incorrect
option_judgments.justification: Internal short circuit is caused by separator shrinkage at ~130°C, not by electrolyte decomposition at 69°C.

analysis.target_cognitive_object: Sequential thermal failure thresholds and their causal mechanisms in 18650 NCA cells
analysis.primary_boundary: All thresholds apply specifically to 18650 NCA cells under thermal abuse; sequence ordering is evidence-anchored
disallowed_extrapolations: Generalizing these thresholds to other cell formats or chemistries
Inferring that SEI decomposition directly causes internal short circuit

solver_view.stem: A simulation models thermal runaway and jet fire behavior for 18650 NCA cells at three SOC levels: 25%, 50%, and 75%. The vent valve opens at 1.9 MPa internal pressure. During the second venting event (TR), which statements about the relationship between SOC, TR onset temperature, jet fire heat release rate, and vent flow regime are supported by the simulation results?
options: A. TR onset temperature decreases monotonically as SOC increases from 25% to 75%.
B. Peak jet fire heat release rate increases monotonically as SOC increases from 25% to 75%.
C. At 25% SOC, vent flow during TR is subsonic; at 50% and 75% SOC, vent flow is choked.
D. At 75% SOC, TR onset temperature is higher than at 25% SOC because more stored energy stabilizes the cell.
E. Peak heat release rates at 50% and 75% SOC are equal because both exhibit choked flow at the vent.
F. The vent valve opening pressure of 1.9 MPa is independent of SOC, but the resulting flow regime is SOC-dependent.

option_judgments.label: Correct
option_judgments.justification: TR onset temperatures of 226°C, 215°C, 190°C at 25%, 50%, 75% SOC confirm monotonic decrease.

option_judgments.label: Correct
option_judgments.justification: Peak HRR values of 0.16, 0.49, 1.24 kW at 25%, 50%, 75% SOC confirm monotonic increase.

option_judgments.label: Correct
option_judgments.justification: 25% SOC shows peak Mach 0.848 (subsonic); 50% and 75% SOC reach Mach 1.0 (choked flow).

option_judgments.label: Incorrect
option_judgments.justification: Reverses the documented trend; higher SOC yields lower TR onset temperature, not higher.

option_judgments.label: Incorrect
option_judgments.justification: Choked flow sets equal peak velocity, not equal heat release rate; HRR differs (0.49 vs 1.24 kW).

option_judgments.label: Correct
option_judgments.justification: Critical pressure of 1.9 MPa is a fixed cell property; flow regime (subsonic vs choked) varies with SOC.

analysis.target_cognitive_object: SOC-dependent coupling between TR onset temperature, jet fire severity, and vent flow regime
analysis.primary_boundary: Results apply to 18650 NCA cells in this simulation; choked flow sets velocity ceiling, not HRR ceiling
disallowed_extrapolations: Assuming choked flow equalizes heat release rates across SOC levels
Generalizing SOC-TR onset trend to other chemistries

solver_view.stem: A thermal runaway simulation of 18650 NCA cells omits radiative heat loss from solid particles and soot in the jet fire model. An engineer uses the simulation's jet fire temperature outputs to assess hazard distances. Which statements correctly characterize the validity and limitations of this approach?
options: A. Jet fire temperatures predicted by the simulation are likely overestimated by 20–40% due to the omission of particle and soot radiation.
B. Hazard distances derived from the simulation's temperature field may be conservatively overestimated, not underestimated.
C. The omission of soot radiation has no effect on temperature predictions because soot is a minor combustion product.
D. The simulation's temperature overestimation means actual jet fire temperatures could be 20–40% lower than predicted.
E. Because the vent valve opens at a fixed pressure of 1.9 MPa regardless of soot modeling, jet fire onset timing is unaffected by this omission.
F. The 20–40% overestimation applies universally to all battery chemistries and cell formats, not just 18650 NCA cells.

option_judgments.label: Correct
option_judgments.justification: Neglecting particle and soot radiation is explicitly documented to cause 20–40% over-prediction in jet fire temperatures.

option_judgments.label: Correct
option_judgments.justification: Overestimated temperatures yield conservative (larger) hazard distances, biasing safety assessment toward over-caution.

option_judgments.label: Incorrect
option_judgments.justification: The evidence explicitly states this omission causes 20–40% over-prediction, contradicting the claim of negligible effect.

option_judgments.label: Correct
option_judgments.justification: If simulation over-predicts by 20–40%, actual temperatures are correspondingly lower than simulated values.

option_judgments.label: Correct
option_judgments.justification: Vent valve opening is governed by internal pressure (1.9 MPa), a mechanical threshold unaffected by jet fire radiation modeling.

option_judgments.label: Incorrect
option_judgments.justification: The 20–40% figure is scoped to this simulation; extending it universally to all chemistries is an unsupported generalization.

analysis.target_cognitive_object: Admissibility of simulation temperature outputs given documented radiation omission and its directional effect on hazard assessment
analysis.primary_boundary: 20–40% overestimation is specific to this simulation configuration; vent pressure threshold is independent of radiation modeling
disallowed_extrapolations: Applying the 20–40% range to other cell chemistries or formats
Inferring that radiation omission affects vent valve timing

solver_view.prompt: For 18650 NCA cells undergoing thermal runaway under thermal abuse, explain why a cell at 75% SOC poses a greater jet fire hazard than a cell at 25% SOC, even though the 75% SOC cell reaches thermal runaway onset at a lower temperature. Your answer should address TR onset temperature, peak heat release rate, and vent flow regime during the second venting event.

ground_truth.gold_answer: Although the 75% SOC cell has a lower TR onset temperature (190°C vs. 226°C at 25% SOC), meaning it enters thermal runaway more readily, the hazard severity once TR occurs is substantially greater. The peak jet fire heat release rate at 75% SOC is 1.24 kW, compared to 0.16 kW at 25% SOC—nearly an eightfold increase. Additionally, the 75% SOC cell produces choked vent flow (Mach 1.0, peak velocity 178.3 m/s) during the second venting event, while the 25% SOC cell remains subsonic (Mach 0.848, 148.1 m/s). Choked flow indicates higher mass flux and momentum of ejected gases, amplifying fire intensity. Thus, higher SOC lowers the thermal threshold for TR onset but dramatically increases the energy and momentum of the resulting jet fire.
must_include: TR onset temperature decreases with increasing SOC (190°C at 75% vs. 226°C at 25%)
Peak heat release rate increases with SOC (1.24 kW at 75% vs. 0.16 kW at 25%)
75% SOC produces choked flow (Mach 1.0); 25% SOC produces subsonic flow (Mach 0.848)
Higher SOC means lower ignition threshold but greater fire severity once TR occurs
disallowed_extrapolations: Claiming choked flow alone determines heat release rate magnitude
Generalizing this SOC-hazard relationship to non-NCA chemistries or non-18650 formats
Asserting that the 1.9 MPa vent pressure threshold changes with SOC

analysis.target_cognitive_object: Multi-variable SOC-dependent hazard reasoning combining TR onset temperature, heat release rate, and vent flow regime
analysis.primary_boundary: Results scoped to 18650 NCA cells in this simulation; choked flow sets velocity ceiling but does not equalize HRR across SOC levels
intended_error_modes: mechanism substitution (conflating choked flow with equal HRR)
unsupported monotonicity (assuming lower onset temperature always means greater hazard)
scale transfer overclaim (generalizing to other chemistries)

solver_view.stem: An 18650-type Li(Ni0.5Co0.2Mn0.3)O2/graphite cell undergoes controlled heating in a sealed canister. The cell is at 100% SOC. Researchers observe the safety valve opening event and later record peak canister pressure just before O-ring seal collapse during full thermal runaway. Which of the following statements about these two pressure events are supported by experimental evidence?
options: A. The pressure increase at safety valve opening is approximately 100 kPa, corresponding to a gas release of roughly 10 mmol.
B. The peak canister pressure before O-ring collapse at 100% SOC is approximately 2.566 MPa, roughly 2.5 times higher than the 0% SOC peak of 1.047 MPa.
C. The safety valve opens at a higher temperature for 100% SOC cells than for 0% SOC cells, because elevated SOC increases internal pressure buildup rate.
D. Peak canister pressure before O-ring collapse is independent of SOC because gas generation is governed solely by electrolyte decomposition.
E. The pressure rise at safety valve opening (~100 kPa) is of the same order of magnitude as the peak pressure before O-ring collapse (~2.566 MPa) at 100% SOC.

option_judgments.label: Correct
option_judgments.justification: Evidence directly states ~100 kPa pressure increase and ~10 mmol gas at safety valve opening.

option_judgments.label: Correct
option_judgments.justification: Evidence states 100% SOC peak is 2.566 MPa and 0% SOC is 1.047 MPa, a ~2.45x ratio.

option_judgments.label: Incorrect
option_judgments.justification: Evidence shows higher SOC yields lower opening temperature (~143.9°C), not higher; direction is reversed.

option_judgments.label: Incorrect
option_judgments.justification: Evidence shows peak pressure is strongly SOC-dependent; SOC-independence claim is unsupported and contradicted.

option_judgments.label: Incorrect
option_judgments.justification: 100 kPa and 2566 kPa differ by ~25x; they are not the same order of magnitude.

analysis.target_cognitive_object: Distinguishing safety valve opening pressure event from peak thermal runaway canister pressure, and SOC dependence of each
analysis.primary_boundary: 100 kPa at valve opening vs. ~2.566 MPa at O-ring collapse; SOC lowers valve opening temperature
disallowed_extrapolations: Generalizing SOC-independence of peak pressure
Equating valve-opening pressure scale to TR peak pressure scale

solver_view.stem: A researcher models gas generation kinetics in 18650-type NCM/graphite cells across Stages II and III of thermal abuse. For 100% SOC cells, Stage III begins at 188.6°C; for 50% SOC cells it begins at 211.6°C. The activation energies of gas generation (Eg) and heat generation (Eh) in Stage III at 100% SOC are 1.718×10^5 J/mol and 1.930×10^5 J/mol, respectively. Which conclusions are supported by this evidence?
options: A. The Stage II–III transition temperature is SOC-dependent, with higher SOC cells transitioning at lower temperatures.
B. The close values of Eg and Eh at 100% SOC suggest gas and heat generation in Stage III share a common underlying reaction mechanism.
C. Because Eg < Eh at 100% SOC, gas generation is always faster than heat generation across all temperatures in Stage III.
D. The Stage II–III transition corresponds to onset of cathode-electrolyte reactions, not anode-related processes.
E. The proportionality between gas generation rate and temperature increase rate means temperature measurement alone can serve as a practical proxy for gas generation simulation.
F. The activation energy relationship Eg ≈ Eh established at 100% SOC can be directly extended to 0% SOC cells without further validation.

option_judgments.label: Correct
option_judgments.justification: 100% SOC transitions at 188.6°C and 50% SOC at 211.6°C; higher SOC yields lower transition temperature.

option_judgments.label: Correct
option_judgments.justification: Evidence explicitly states close Eg and Eh values indicate an inherent link between gas and heat generation processes.

option_judgments.label: Incorrect
option_judgments.justification: Activation energy inequality does not imply rate ordering without knowing pre-exponential factors; this is a causal overclaim.

option_judgments.label: Correct
option_judgments.justification: Evidence states the Stage II–III transition temperature agrees with cathode-electrolyte reaction onset temperature.

option_judgments.label: Correct
option_judgments.justification: Evidence states gas generation rate is approximately proportional to temperature increase rate, facilitating simulation via temperature measurement.

option_judgments.label: Incorrect
option_judgments.justification: Evidence establishes Eg ≈ Eh only for 100% SOC; extending to 0% SOC is an unsupported regime transfer.

analysis.target_cognitive_object: SOC-dependent kinetic transition temperatures, activation energy interpretation, and scope of proportionality claim
analysis.primary_boundary: Eg ≈ Eh finding is restricted to 100% SOC Stage III; transition temperature is SOC-dependent
disallowed_extrapolations: Extending Eg/Eh relationship to other SOC levels
Using Eg < Eh alone to rank reaction rates

solver_view.stem: During a thermal runaway experiment on a sealed 18650-type NCM/graphite cell, Stage IV begins when the battery bursts and hot gases eject rapidly into the canister. A modeler attempts to apply the same gas-temperature equation used in Stages II and III to calculate Stage IV kinetics parameters. Which statements correctly identify why this approach is problematic or what valid alternatives exist?
options: A. The gas temperature equation is inapplicable in Stage IV because turbulent, heterogeneous temperature distribution inside the canister violates the equation's underlying assumption.
B. The gas temperature equation fails in Stage IV primarily because the activation energy of gas generation changes discontinuously at thermal runaway onset.
C. Because gas generation rate is proportional to temperature increase rate across all SOC conditions, the Stage IV kinetics can still be estimated from thermocouple data alone.
D. Stage IV kinetics parameters cannot be reliably extracted using the same equation, making Stage IV a defined modeling boundary in this experimental framework.
E. The inapplicability of the gas temperature equation in Stage IV is caused by the O-ring seal collapse, which changes the system boundary from closed to open.

option_judgments.label: Correct
option_judgments.justification: Evidence explicitly states turbulent, heterogeneous gas temperature distribution in Stage IV invalidates the equation assumption.

option_judgments.label: Incorrect
option_judgments.justification: Evidence attributes failure to temperature heterogeneity, not a discontinuous activation energy change; mechanism substitution error.

option_judgments.label: Incorrect
option_judgments.justification: Proportionality claim applies across SOC conditions but evidence explicitly excludes Stage IV from reliable kinetics calculation.

option_judgments.label: Correct
option_judgments.justification: Evidence states the assumption is not applicable in Stage IV, making it an explicit modeling boundary where kinetics extraction is unreliable.

option_judgments.label: Incorrect
option_judgments.justification: Evidence attributes inapplicability to turbulent temperature heterogeneity inside the canister, not to O-ring collapse changing system boundary.

analysis.target_cognitive_object: Identifying the specific physical reason the gas temperature equation fails in Stage IV and the resulting modeling boundary
analysis.primary_boundary: Stage IV is explicitly excluded from kinetics parameter extraction due to turbulent heterogeneous temperature distribution
disallowed_extrapolations: Using Stage II–III proportionality to rescue Stage IV calculations
Attributing failure to activation energy discontinuity or seal geometry

solver_view.prompt: For 18650-type NCM/graphite cells tested across 0%, 50%, and 100% SOC, explain how SOC affects two distinct thermal threshold temperatures: (1) the safety valve opening temperature and (2) the Stage II–III gas generation transition temperature. For each, identify the physical mechanism responsible for the SOC dependence according to experimental evidence. Then state one explicit modeling limitation that prevents extending the gas generation kinetics framework to Stage IV regardless of SOC.

ground_truth.gold_answer: Safety valve opening temperature decreases as SOC increases (from ~155.5°C at 0% SOC to ~143.9°C at 100% SOC) because higher SOC elevates the thermal activity of anode-electrolyte and cathode materials, causing earlier internal pressure buildup. The Stage II–III transition temperature also decreases with increasing SOC (211.6°C at 50% SOC vs. 188.6°C at 100% SOC), corresponding to earlier onset of cathode-electrolyte reactions at higher states of charge. Both thresholds shift downward with SOC but via related yet distinct reaction pathways. The Stage IV modeling limitation is that when thermal runaway occurs, ejected hot gases create turbulent, thermally heterogeneous conditions inside the canister, violating the gas temperature assumption required for kinetics parameter extraction, making Stage IV an explicit boundary of the framework.
must_include: Safety valve opening temperature decreases with increasing SOC
Higher SOC increases thermal activity of anode-electrolyte and cathode materials
Stage II–III transition temperature decreases with increasing SOC
Transition corresponds to cathode-electrolyte reaction onset
Stage IV excluded due to turbulent heterogeneous temperature distribution invalidating gas temperature equation
disallowed_extrapolations: Claiming safety valve opening temperature is SOC-independent
Attributing Stage II–III transition to anode reactions alone
Claiming Stage IV kinetics can be estimated from thermocouple data via the proportionality relationship
Extending Eg ≈ Eh finding from 100% SOC to all SOC conditions as a universal result

analysis.target_cognitive_object: Integrating SOC effects on two separate thermal thresholds with their distinct physical mechanisms, and identifying the Stage IV modeling boundary
analysis.primary_boundary: Both thresholds are SOC-dependent but governed by different reaction onsets; Stage IV is outside the kinetics framework due to temperature heterogeneity

solver_view.stem: A 27 Ah prismatic LiFePO4 battery undergoes full thermal runaway. Simulation results show total mass loss of ~140 g split among ejected electrolyte vapours, generated reaction gases, and solid particles. An engineer reviews gas-release timing and composition across the pre-TR and TR stages. Which of the following statements are supported by the evidence?
options: A. Electrolyte vapours dominate gas release before thermal runaway, but are essentially absent during the thermal runaway stage itself because liquid electrolyte is nearly fully consumed beforehand.
B. Generated reaction gases dominate gas release before thermal runaway because exothermic decomposition reactions begin well ahead of the burst of chain reactions.
C. Solid particles represent the second-largest mass-loss fraction overall, contributing approximately 38% of total ejected mass, exceeding generated reaction gases.
D. Electrolyte vapours and generated reaction gases together account for more than 80% of total mass loss, making solid particles a minor contributor.
E. The transition from electrolyte-vapour dominance to reaction-gas dominance coincides with the burst of chain reactions during thermal runaway.
F. Generated reaction gases account for the largest single fraction of total mass loss at approximately 50.5%.

option_judgments.label: Correct
option_judgments.justification: Evidence states electrolyte is almost wholly transformed before TR, so no vapours are ejected during TR stage itself.

option_judgments.label: Incorrect
option_judgments.justification: Evidence states reaction gases become major release after chain reactions burst, not before; this reverses the reported temporal trend.

option_judgments.label: Correct
option_judgments.justification: Solid particles: 53.83 g (38.4%); generated gases: 15.44 g (11.1%). Particles are second-largest fraction, exceeding reaction gases.

option_judgments.label: Incorrect
option_judgments.justification: Vapours (50.5%) + gases (11.1%) = 61.6%, not >80%. Solid particles (38.4%) are not minor; this overclaims vapour+gas dominance.

option_judgments.label: Correct
option_judgments.justification: Evidence directly links the shift from vapour dominance to reaction-gas dominance with the burst of chain reactions.

option_judgments.label: Incorrect
option_judgments.justification: Electrolyte vapours (50.5%) are the largest fraction; generated gases are only 11.1%. Mechanism substitution error.

analysis.target_cognitive_object: Temporal phasing of gas-release composition and quantitative mass-fraction ranking across TR stages
analysis.primary_boundary: Pre-TR vs. TR stage distinction; electrolyte fully consumed before TR onset
disallowed_extrapolations: Generalising LiFePO4 mass fractions to other chemistries
Assuming reaction-gas dominance applies pre-TR

solver_view.stem: A battery pack experiences thermal runaway in one cell. Engineers assess fire-propagation risk to adjacent cells and inhalation hazard from ejected particles. Ventilation rate can be varied between 100 and 300 L/h. Based on the described physical mechanisms and measured data, which statements correctly characterise particle behaviour and hazard management?
options: A. Ejected high-temperature particles from a TR cell can ignite combustible gases released by neighbouring cells, acting as an ignition medium for fire propagation.
B. Increasing ventilation from 100 to 300 L/h eliminates inhalation hazard because all suspended particles are exhausted within the ventilation period.
C. Particles smaller than 10 µm diameter can remain suspended in air for more than 2000 seconds after TR venting, posing a prolonged inhalation hazard.
D. Increasing ventilation from 100 to 300 L/h raises the fraction of total particle mass discharged via exhaust from approximately 17.5% to 32.0% while reducing peak suspended particle mass.
E. Higher ventilation rates increase peak suspended particle mass because turbulent airflow re-entrains deposited particles from surfaces.
F. Solid particles are a negligible ignition hazard because their mass fraction (~38%) is lower than that of ejected electrolyte vapours (~50%).

option_judgments.label: Correct
option_judgments.justification: Evidence explicitly states ejected high-temperature particles ignite combustible gases from adjacent batteries, driving fire propagation.

option_judgments.label: Incorrect
option_judgments.justification: Evidence shows sub-10 µm particles persist beyond 2000 s even at higher ventilation; elimination claim is an unsupported universal overclaim.

option_judgments.label: Correct
option_judgments.justification: Evidence confirms sub-10 µm particles remain suspended beyond 2000 s, explicitly flagged as prolonged inhalation hazard.

option_judgments.label: Correct
option_judgments.justification: Evidence reports exhaust fraction rises from 17.5% to 32.0% and peak suspended mass falls from 34.6 g to 14.9 g over 100–300 L/h range.

option_judgments.label: Incorrect
option_judgments.justification: Evidence shows peak suspended mass decreases with higher ventilation; this option reverses the reported trend.

option_judgments.label: Incorrect
option_judgments.justification: Ignition hazard depends on temperature and kinetic energy, not mass fraction rank. Attributing negligibility to mass fraction is a mechanism substitution error.

analysis.target_cognitive_object: Particle ignition mechanism, size-dependent suspension persistence, and ventilation trade-offs for hazard mitigation
analysis.primary_boundary: Ventilation range 100–300 L/h; sub-10 µm particle persistence beyond 2000 s; ignition driven by temperature/kinetic energy not mass fraction
disallowed_extrapolations: Claiming ventilation fully eliminates particle hazard
Inferring ignition risk from mass fraction alone

solver_view.stem: For a 27 Ah prismatic LiFePO4 battery, the ejection energy during thermal runaway venting is approximately 47.58 kJ, and total self-heating energy released during thermal runaway is approximately 268.91 kJ. An engineer evaluates energy partitioning and its implications for cell-level thermal models. Which conclusions are directly supported?
options: A. Ejection energy accounts for approximately 17.69% of total self-heating energy released during thermal runaway.
B. Because ejection energy is less than 20% of total TR energy, it can be universally neglected in all thermal runaway models regardless of pack geometry.
C. The majority of thermal runaway self-heating energy (~82%) is retained within or dissipated by the cell rather than carried away by ejected material.
D. Ejection energy exceeds the energy stored in generated reaction gases alone, since reaction gases represent only 11.1% of total mass loss.
E. The 17.69% ejection energy fraction applies equally to all lithium-ion chemistries and capacities because it reflects a fundamental electrochemical ratio.
F. Ejection energy represents a non-negligible fraction of total TR energy and should be considered when accounting for energy balance during thermal runaway.

option_judgments.label: Correct
option_judgments.justification: 47.58 kJ / 268.91 kJ ≈ 17.69% as directly stated in the evidence.

option_judgments.label: Incorrect
option_judgments.justification: Evidence provides this ratio for one specific cell; universally neglecting ejection energy across all models is an unsupported unconditional generalisation.

option_judgments.label: Correct
option_judgments.justification: If ejection carries ~17.69%, then ~82.31% remains; this follows directly from the reported energy partition.

option_judgments.label: Incorrect
option_judgments.justification: Mass fraction of gases (11.1%) cannot be directly compared to ejection energy (kJ) without energy-per-mass data not provided; scale transfer error.

option_judgments.label: Incorrect
option_judgments.justification: Evidence is specific to one 27 Ah LiFePO4 cell; extending the ratio universally across chemistries and capacities is an unsupported generalisation.

option_judgments.label: Correct
option_judgments.justification: 17.69% is a substantial fraction; evidence supports its inclusion in energy balance accounting during TR, consistent with the reported calculation.

analysis.target_cognitive_object: Energy partitioning between ejection and self-heating during TR, and admissibility of generalisation
analysis.primary_boundary: Single 27 Ah LiFePO4 cell; ratio not transferable universally; mass fraction ≠ energy fraction without additional data
disallowed_extrapolations: Applying 17.69% to other chemistries or capacities
Inferring energy magnitude from mass fraction alone

solver_view.prompt: A safety engineer is designing a ventilated enclosure for a battery pack containing 27 Ah prismatic LiFePO4 cells. The enclosure must address both fire-propagation risk between cells and inhalation hazard from ejected particles after a thermal runaway event. Using the physical mechanisms and quantitative data associated with particle ejection, gas release phasing, and ventilation effects, explain: (1) the primary physical mechanism by which thermal runaway in one cell can cause fire propagation to adjacent cells, and (2) why increasing ventilation rate reduces but does not eliminate inhalation hazard, referencing the relevant particle size threshold and suspension timescale.

ground_truth.gold_answer: Fire propagation mechanism: Ejected solid particles from a cell undergoing thermal runaway carry high temperature and kinetic energy. When these particles contact combustible gases released by neighbouring cells (which have not yet reached TR), they act as ignition sources, initiating combustion in adjacent cells. This mechanism is distinct from direct heat conduction or radiation. Ventilation and inhalation hazard: Increasing ventilation from 100 to 300 L/h raises the fraction of total particle mass exhausted from ~17.5% to ~32.0% and reduces peak suspended particle mass from ~34.6 g to ~14.9 g, improving clearance. However, particles smaller than 10 µm diameter remain suspended in air for more than 2000 seconds even after venting ceases, because their low settling velocity prevents gravitational deposition on engineering timescales. Ventilation cannot fully eliminate these fine particles within practical timeframes, so inhalation hazard persists.
must_include: Ejected high-temperature particles ignite combustible gases from adjacent cells as the fire propagation mechanism
Ventilation increases exhaust fraction and reduces peak suspended mass
Sub-10 µm particles remain suspended beyond 2000 seconds
Inhalation hazard is reduced but not eliminated by ventilation
disallowed_extrapolations: Claiming ventilation fully eliminates particle hazard
Attributing fire propagation solely to radiated heat or conduction without referencing particle ignition mechanism
Generalising quantitative ventilation figures to other cell chemistries or enclosure geometries
Inferring that larger particles pose the dominant long-term inhalation risk

analysis.target_cognitive_object: Integration of particle ignition mechanism for fire propagation with size-dependent suspension physics and ventilation trade-off limits
analysis.primary_boundary: Particle ignition mechanism specific to ejected high-temperature particles; sub-10 µm persistence beyond 2000 s; ventilation range 100–300 L/h for one tested configuration
intended_error_modes: mechanism_substitution (heat conduction instead of particle ignition)

solver_view.stem: An MPI-DeepONet model and a purely data-driven DeepONet model are both trained to predict thermal runaway temperature fields in LFP 26650 cells. Training data are progressively thinned so that the time interval between collocation points increases beyond 30 minutes. Which of the following statements are supported by the described experimental evidence?
options: A. Beyond the ~30-minute data interval threshold, DeepONet RMSE increases significantly while MPI-DeepONet RMSE remains comparatively stable.
B. Beyond the ~30-minute threshold, both models degrade at similar rates because sparse data affects all neural operator architectures equally.
C. MPI-DeepONet's relative robustness to sparse data is attributed to supervision from multiphysics constraints including thermodynamics and chemical reaction phenomena.
D. MPI-DeepONet achieves lower RMSE than DeepONet only in the dense-data regime; performance converges when data become sparse.
E. The sparse-data robustness of MPI-DeepONet implies it requires no training data at all and can operate purely from physics equations.
F. MPI-DeepONet outperforms DeepONet across all tested data densities, not only in the sparse regime.

option_judgments.label: Correct
option_judgments.justification: Evidence states DeepONet RMSE increases significantly as data become sparse beyond ~30 min while MPI-DeepONet remains stable.

option_judgments.label: Incorrect
option_judgments.justification: Evidence shows divergent degradation rates; the two models do not degrade equally. This reverses the reported differential trend.

option_judgments.label: Correct
option_judgments.justification: Evidence explicitly attributes MPI-DeepONet's sparse-data robustness to multiphysics supervision covering thermodynamics and chemical reactions.

option_judgments.label: Incorrect
option_judgments.justification: Evidence states MPI-DeepONet outperforms DeepONet in all cases, not only in dense-data conditions. Convergence claim is unsupported.

option_judgments.label: Incorrect
option_judgments.justification: Robustness to sparse data does not imply zero data requirement. This is an unsupported extrapolation beyond the evidence scope.

option_judgments.label: Correct
option_judgments.justification: Evidence states MPI-DeepONet predicts more accurately than DeepONet in all tested cases, covering all data densities.

analysis.target_cognitive_object: Condition-dependent robustness of physics-informed vs. data-driven neural operators under sparse training data
analysis.primary_boundary: Sparse-data regime defined by data time interval exceeding ~30 minutes for LFP 26650 cells
disallowed_extrapolations: Generalizing sparse-data robustness to zero-data operation
Claiming performance convergence between models at high sparsity

solver_view.stem: A thermal runaway prediction framework is benchmarked on two cell chemistries: LFP 26650 and NMC 18650. The multiphysics FEM requires 7 s for LFP and 75 s for NMC. MPI-DeepONet with encoders requires 0.5 ms for both. An engineer claims the speed advantage of MPI-DeepONet is 'approximately four orders of magnitude for both chemistries.' Which options correctly evaluate this claim and the underlying speed comparison evidence?
options: A. The claim is incorrect for NMC 18650; the speed advantage is five orders of magnitude, not four, because FEM requires 75 s versus 0.5 ms.
B. The claim is correct for LFP 26650 because 7 s versus 0.5 ms corresponds to approximately four orders of magnitude difference.
C. The claim is correct for NMC 18650 because the absolute time saved (74.9995 s) is larger than for LFP, implying a greater practical advantage regardless of order-of-magnitude classification.
D. MPI-DeepONet prediction time scales with cell chemistry complexity, so NMC predictions inherently take longer than LFP predictions.
E. The consistent 0.5 ms MPI-DeepONet prediction time across both chemistries indicates the model's inference cost is independent of the specific cell type tested.
