B. LiCoO2 offers greater thermal stability than NCA, making it preferable for high-temperature stationary applications.
C. Four acoustic sensors at station corners using sound source localization can locate a faulty cell with maximum spatial error of 0.1 m and millisecond-level response.
D. Acoustic sensor localization accuracy of 0.1 m demonstrated in one energy storage station configuration can be directly applied to all station geometries without recalibration.
E. Mechanical abuse such as nail penetration causes an abrupt voltage drop to 0 V, whereas electrical or thermal abuse produces more gradual, complex voltage declines.
F. Because voltage signals for thermal abuse are gradual, they provide equivalent early-warning capability to acoustic sensors for all abuse modes.

option_judgments.label: Correct
option_judgments.justification: Directly matches the stated thermal stability order: LFP > LMO > NCM(x≤0.8) > NCA > LCO.

option_judgments.label: Incorrect
option_judgments.justification: Reverses reported order: NCA is more thermally stable than LCO; LCO is the least stable in the reported ranking.

option_judgments.label: Correct
option_judgments.justification: Supported: four-corner acoustic sensor placement with localization achieved ≤0.1 m spatial error and millisecond response times.

option_judgments.label: Incorrect
option_judgments.justification: Scale transfer overclaim: the 0.1 m result is from one specific station configuration; generalization to all geometries is unsupported.

option_judgments.label: Correct
option_judgments.justification: Directly supported: nail penetration → abrupt 0 V drop; electrical/thermal abuse → gradual, complex voltage decline.

option_judgments.label: Incorrect
option_judgments.justification: Causal overclaim: gradual voltage signals for thermal abuse have poor temporal resolution; equivalence to acoustic sensors is unsupported.

analysis.target_cognitive_object: Cathode thermal stability ranking and acoustic sensor localization capability with scope boundaries
analysis.primary_boundary: Acoustic localization result is configuration-specific; LCO is least stable, not intermediate
disallowed_extrapolations: Universal acoustic localization accuracy
LCO superiority over NCA
Voltage signal equivalence to acoustic detection for thermal abuse

solver_view.prompt: A lithium-ion battery cell progresses through a thermal runaway sequence starting from normal operation. Describe the ordered sequence of thermally-triggered events and associated temperature thresholds, identifying which gas species are generated at each stage and explaining why the flammability hazard profile depends on electrolyte solvent composition. Also explain why a battery management system relying solely on voltage and impedance monitoring may fail to provide adequate early warning, and what adjustment is required for alarm thresholds as cell SOH degrades.

ground_truth.gold_answer: The thermal runaway sequence begins with SEI layer decomposition at 80–120 °C, releasing CO2 and C2H4. At ~120 °C the SEI is nearly fully decomposed, exposing the anode directly to electrolyte; exothermic reactions generate flammable alkanes with intensity proportional to SOC. The separator (PE or PP) melts at ~130–170 °C, sharply increasing internal resistance and risking internal short circuits. Commercial electrolyte decomposes at ~150 °C, with PF5 byproducts accelerating breakdown and reacting with H2O to release toxic HF. Above 260 °C, binders react with intercalated lithium to produce H2. At ~300 °C, large-scale flammable gas generation causes swelling; full thermal runaway reaches 500–1000 °C internally. Flammability hazard depends on solvent: DMC/EC/EMC electrolytes yield up to 70% non-flammable CO2, while DEC-based electrolytes yield predominantly flammable H2, C2H6, C3H8. Voltage and impedance signals change more slowly than thermal runaway propagation, giving poor temporal resolution and delayed warning. As SOH degrades (rising internal resistance, active material loss), the self-heating onset temperature decreases, so alarm thresholds must be dynamically adjusted downward to maintain safety margins.
must_include: SEI decomposition at 80–120 °C releasing CO2 and C2H4
Anode-electrolyte exothermic reactions at ~120 °C producing flammable alkanes, intensity correlated with SOC
Separator melting at 130–170 °C sharply increasing internal resistance
Electrolyte decomposition at ~150 °C with HF release
Binder H2 generation above 260 °C
Solvent-dependent flammability: DMC/EC/EMC yields predominantly CO2; DEC yields flammable gases
Voltage/impedance poor temporal resolution relative to thermal runaway propagation rate
SOH degradation lowers self-heating onset temperature, requiring dynamic downward threshold adjustment
disallowed_extrapolations: Claiming CO2 dominance is universal across all electrolyte formulations
Claiming voltage signals provide equivalent early warning to gas or acoustic sensors
Claiming static alarm thresholds remain adequate as SOH degrades
Claiming separator melting reduces internal resistance or aids ion transport
Generalizing the 520 °C internal temperature difference to all cell formats or chemistries

analysis.target_cognitive_object: Ordered multi-stage thermal runaway sequence with threshold-specific gas generation, solvent-conditional flammability, sensor modality limitations, and SOH-adaptive alarm design
analysis.primary_boundary: Flammability profile is electrolyte-solvent-conditional; SOH effect is directionally specific (lower onset); voltage/impedance limitation is relative to propagation rate, not absolute

solver_view.stem: An NCA/Graphite 18650 cell is cycled 30 times at -10°C, reducing SOH to approximately 47%. During a subsequent thermal runaway event, which of the following statements correctly describe the thermal behavior of this aged cell compared to a fresh cell? Select all that apply.
options: A. Thermal runaway initiates at a lower temperature (~140°C) due to plated lithium reacting with electrolyte.
B. Total heat generation during thermal runaway is substantially higher than in a fresh cell because of accumulated lithium plating.
C. The positive-electrolyte reaction remains the dominant heat source, contributing the largest share of total heat generation.
D. Lithium plating and SEI thickening both independently worsen thermal runaway onset temperature.
E. The negative-electrolyte reaction is the second largest heat source, and together with the positive-electrolyte reaction accounts for over 90% of total heat.

option_judgments.label: Correct
option_judgments.justification: Plated lithium reacts with electrolyte at ~140°C in low-temperature cycled cells, causing earlier TR onset.

option_judgments.label: Incorrect
option_judgments.justification: Total heat generation increases only a little with ageing state; a substantial increase is unsupported.

option_judgments.label: Correct
option_judgments.justification: Positive-electrolyte reaction is the largest heat source during TR regardless of ageing state.

option_judgments.label: Incorrect
option_judgments.justification: SEI thickening improves thermal stability; only lithium plating worsens TR. The two effects are opposite, not both detrimental.

option_judgments.label: Correct
option_judgments.justification: Negative-electrolyte reaction is second largest; combined with positive-electrolyte reaction they exceed 90% of total heat.

analysis.target_cognitive_object: Distinguishing ageing-induced TR onset shift from total heat generation change, and correctly attributing dominant heat sources.
analysis.primary_boundary: Total heat increases only marginally with ageing; earlier onset is driven by lithium plating reaction, not a large energy increase.
disallowed_extrapolations: Claiming substantial total heat increase due to ageing
Claiming SEI thickening worsens TR onset

solver_view.stem: A researcher claims that because SEI film growth and lithium plating both accumulate during low-temperature cycling, they must both degrade thermal runaway safety in NCA/Graphite cells. Which of the following options correctly identify errors or valid counter-points to this claim? Select all that apply.
options: A. The claim is wrong because SEI film thickening actually improves battery thermal stability, not degrades it.
B. The claim is partially correct: lithium plating does degrade TR performance, but SEI thickening has the opposite effect on safety.
C. The claim is correct because both mechanisms reduce available capacity, which always worsens thermal runaway severity.
D. Lithium plating promotes earlier TR onset by enabling an exothermic reaction with electrolyte near 140°C, supporting the degradation claim only for that mechanism.
E. Since total heat generation increases only marginally with ageing, neither SEI growth nor lithium plating significantly affects thermal runaway energy release.
F. The claim conflates two mechanisms with opposite safety effects; SEI and lithium plating must be evaluated separately.

option_judgments.label: Correct
option_judgments.justification: SEI thickening improves thermal stability; the researcher's blanket degradation claim is therefore incorrect for this mechanism.

option_judgments.label: Correct
option_judgments.justification: Lithium plating deteriorates TR performance while SEI thickening improves it—opposite effects, confirming partial correctness only.

option_judgments.label: Incorrect
option_judgments.justification: Capacity reduction does not universally worsen TR severity; this is an unsupported causal overclaim not grounded in evidence.

option_judgments.label: Correct
option_judgments.justification: Plated lithium reacts with electrolyte at ~140°C, causing earlier TR onset, validating the degradation claim specifically for lithium plating.

option_judgments.label: Incorrect
option_judgments.justification: Marginal total heat change does not imply neither mechanism affects safety; onset temperature is separately affected by lithium plating.

option_judgments.label: Correct
option_judgments.justification: SEI and lithium plating have opposite safety effects and must not be conflated into a single directional claim.

analysis.target_cognitive_object: Evaluating the directional safety effects of two co-occurring ageing mechanisms and identifying unsupported generalizations.
analysis.primary_boundary: SEI thickening and lithium plating have opposite effects on TR safety; they cannot be treated as uniformly detrimental.
disallowed_extrapolations: Capacity loss universally predicts TR severity
Marginal heat change implies no safety impact from ageing mechanisms

solver_view.stem: Two NCA/Graphite 18650 cells are aged by cycling at -10°C: Cell A for 10 cycles (SOH ≈ 83%) and Cell B for 30 cycles (SOH ≈ 47%). Both undergo thermal runaway testing. Which statements about comparing their thermal runaway characteristics are INCORRECT? Select all that apply.
options: A. Cell B will release dramatically more total heat than Cell A because its SOH is nearly half that of Cell A.
B. Both cells may exhibit earlier TR onset than a fresh cell due to lithium plating reacting with electrolyte near 140°C.
C. The positive-electrolyte reaction will no longer dominate heat generation in Cell B because lithium plating becomes the primary heat source at severe ageing.
D. Cell B's greater capacity loss guarantees a proportionally lower TR onset temperature than Cell A.
E. In both cells, the combined heat from positive-electrolyte and negative-electrolyte reactions is expected to exceed 90% of total heat generation.

option_judgments.label: Incorrect (selected as wrong statement)
option_judgments.justification: Total heat generation increases only marginally with ageing state; a dramatic increase for Cell B is unsupported.

option_judgments.label: Correct (not a wrong statement)
option_judgments.justification: Both cells were cycled at -10°C and likely have plated lithium; the ~140°C reaction applies to low-temperature cycled cells generally.

option_judgments.label: Incorrect (selected as wrong statement)
option_judgments.justification: Positive-electrolyte reaction remains the largest heat source; no evidence supports lithium plating displacing it at severe ageing.

option_judgments.label: Incorrect (selected as wrong statement)
option_judgments.justification: Proportional TR onset reduction with capacity loss is not supported; the ~140°C threshold is reported, not a continuous SOH-dependent shift.

option_judgments.label: Correct (not a wrong statement)
option_judgments.justification: The >90% combined share of positive- and negative-electrolyte reactions is stated as a general TR characteristic, applicable to both cells.

analysis.target_cognitive_object: Identifying unsupported extrapolations from SOH differences to TR heat magnitude and onset temperature scaling.
analysis.primary_boundary: Total heat increases only marginally with ageing; dominant heat source hierarchy is preserved; onset temperature is not proportionally linked to SOH.
disallowed_extrapolations: Proportional TR heat scaling with SOH
Lithium plating displacing positive-electrolyte reaction as dominant heat source

solver_view.prompt: An NCA/Graphite 18650 cell is subjected to repeated low-temperature cycling at -10°C. Two ageing products accumulate over cycles: thickened SEI film and plated lithium. A safety engineer argues that both products worsen thermal runaway risk equally and that more cycling always produces proportionally more dangerous thermal runaway events. Using the evidence available, evaluate this argument. Address: (1) whether the two ageing products have the same directional effect on thermal runaway safety, (2) whether total heat generation scales proportionally with ageing severity, and (3) what specific mechanism drives earlier thermal runaway onset in these cells.

ground_truth.gold_answer: The engineer's argument is incorrect on multiple points. First, SEI thickening and lithium plating have opposite effects on safety: SEI thickening improves battery thermal stability, while lithium plating greatly deteriorates thermal runaway performance. They cannot be treated as equally detrimental. Second, total heat generation during thermal runaway increases only marginally with ageing state for low-temperature cycled cells, so proportional scaling with cycling severity is not supported. Third, the mechanism driving earlier TR onset is the exothermic reaction between plated lithium and electrolyte, which occurs at approximately 140°C in low-temperature cycled cells, causing thermal runaway to begin at a lower temperature than in fresh cells.
must_include: SEI thickening improves thermal stability (opposite direction to lithium plating)
Lithium plating deteriorates thermal runaway performance
Total heat generation increases only marginally with ageing state
Plated lithium reacts with electrolyte at approximately 140°C causing earlier TR onset
disallowed_extrapolations: Claiming total heat generation scales proportionally or substantially with SOH degradation
Claiming SEI thickening worsens thermal runaway
Claiming lithium plating becomes the dominant heat source replacing positive-electrolyte reaction
Generalizing the 140°C onset to non-low-temperature cycling conditions

analysis.target_cognitive_object: Multi-mechanism safety evaluation: distinguishing directional effects of co-occurring ageing products and separating TR onset temperature shift from total heat generation scaling.
analysis.primary_boundary: SEI and lithium plating have opposite safety effects; total heat change is marginal; earlier onset is mechanism-specific to plated lithium at ~140°C.

solver_view.stem: A lithium-ion battery module undergoes mechanical compression testing in two separate trials: one compressing along the thickness direction and one along the width direction. Both trials use the same cell chemistry and loading rate. An engineer assesses thermal runaway risk and heat management outcome for each direction. Which of the following statements are correct regarding these two compression scenarios?
options: A. Thickness-direction compression produces a more structurally stable module but creates greater thermal runaway risk due to heat accumulation.
B. Width-direction compression is more dangerous because fracture is less likely, limiting heat dissipation pathways.
C. Fracture occurring in the width direction facilitates timely heat release, reducing thermal runaway risk relative to thickness-direction impact.
D. Thickness-direction compression is safer overall because the module structure remains intact and no fracture pathways form.
E. Both directions pose equal thermal runaway risk because structural stability and heat accumulation are independent variables.
F. The direction of compression does not influence thermal runaway risk when loading rate and cell chemistry are held constant.

option_judgments.label: Correct
option_judgments.justification: Thickness direction yields greater structural stability but heat accumulates, increasing thermal runaway risk per evidence.

option_judgments.label: Incorrect
option_judgments.justification: Reverses the evidence: width-direction fracture aids heat release, making it less dangerous, not more.

option_judgments.label: Correct
option_judgments.justification: Evidence states fracture in length/width directions releases heat timely, reducing thermal runaway risk.

option_judgments.label: Incorrect
option_judgments.justification: Structural integrity in thickness direction is conflated with safety; heat accumulation makes it more dangerous, not safer.

option_judgments.label: Incorrect
option_judgments.justification: Evidence shows structural stability and heat accumulation interact, producing direction-dependent risk differences.

option_judgments.label: Incorrect
option_judgments.justification: Compression direction is explicitly shown to influence heat release pathways and thermal runaway risk.

analysis.target_cognitive_object: Interaction between structural fracture behavior and heat dissipation pathway as a function of compression direction
analysis.primary_boundary: Applies only to module-level compression in thickness vs. length/width directions under the described mechanical abuse scenario
disallowed_extrapolations: Generalizing direction-dependent risk to all loading rates or chemistries
Concluding structural stability alone determines safety

solver_view.stem: An engineer compares thermal runaway propagation mechanisms in two battery module designs: one using cylindrical cells and one using pouch cells, both operating under identical thermal abuse conditions. The pouch cells have substantially larger inter-cell contact area than the cylindrical cells. Which of the following statements correctly describe the dominant propagation pathways in each module type?
options: A. In cylindrical cell modules, thermal runaway propagates mainly through electrical connections rather than direct cell-to-cell heat transfer.
B. In pouch cell modules, thermal runaway propagates primarily through electrical connections because pouch cells have lower internal resistance.
C. In pouch cell modules, heat transfer between adjacent cells dominates propagation due to the large inter-cell contact area.
D. Both module types share the same dominant propagation mechanism because thermal runaway physics are chemistry-independent.
E. Cylindrical cell modules propagate thermal runaway faster than pouch modules because electrical connections conduct heat more efficiently than surface contact.
F. The larger contact area of pouch cells shifts the dominant propagation mode from electrical conduction to inter-cell heat transfer.

option_judgments.label: Correct
option_judgments.justification: Evidence states cylindrical battery modules transmit heat mainly through electrical connections.

option_judgments.label: Incorrect
option_judgments.justification: Attributes cylindrical module mechanism to pouch modules; evidence states pouch modules are dominated by direct heat transfer.

option_judgments.label: Correct
option_judgments.justification: Evidence explicitly states pouch battery modules are dominated by heat transfer between batteries.

option_judgments.label: Incorrect
option_judgments.justification: Evidence demonstrates module geometry and contact area produce distinct propagation mechanisms across cell formats.

option_judgments.label: Incorrect
option_judgments.justification: Comparative propagation speed is not supported by evidence; only dominant pathway differs, not speed ranking.

option_judgments.label: Correct
option_judgments.justification: Evidence links larger pouch contact area directly to heat-transfer-dominated propagation as the causal mechanism.

analysis.target_cognitive_object: Causal link between cell format contact geometry and dominant thermal runaway propagation pathway
analysis.primary_boundary: Applies to cylindrical vs. pouch module comparison under identical thermal abuse; speed comparison not supported
disallowed_extrapolations: Ranking absolute propagation speed across formats
Extending findings to other cell geometries without evidence

solver_view.stem: During post-mortem analysis of a mechanically abused lithium-ion cell, four internal short-circuit contact modes are identified. An engineer must rank them by electrocaloric danger and also assess whether a multi-point annular short circuit or a single-point short circuit poses greater thermal risk. Which of the following statements are NOT supported by the available evidence?
options: A. Contact between anode material coating and cathode current collector (Al) is the most dangerous internal short-circuit mode electrocalorically.
B. A single-point short circuit accumulates heat faster and raises temperature more rapidly than a multi-point annular short circuit.
C. A multi-point annular short circuit accumulates heat more easily and raises battery temperature more rapidly than a single-point short circuit.
D. All four internal short-circuit modes produce identical electrocaloric responses regardless of the materials in contact.
E. The anode-cathode current collector contact mode is dangerous because it involves the Al current collector, which has lower resistance than other contact pairs.
F. Separator fracture under mechanical abuse is a necessary precondition for internal short circuit formation.

option_judgments.label: Incorrect (supported)
option_judgments.justification: Evidence explicitly identifies anode coating–cathode Al current collector contact as the most dangerous mode.

option_judgments.label: Correct (not supported)
option_judgments.justification: Reverses the evidence-supported finding; multi-point annular is more dangerous, not single-point.

option_judgments.label: Incorrect (supported)
option_judgments.justification: Evidence directly states multi-point annular short circuit accumulates heat more easily and raises temperature faster.

option_judgments.label: Correct (not supported)
option_judgments.justification: Evidence states the four modes differ in electrocaloric response; identical responses are explicitly contradicted.

option_judgments.label: Correct (not supported)
option_judgments.justification: Evidence identifies the mode as most dangerous but does not attribute this to Al resistance; this is an unsupported mechanistic addition.

option_judgments.label: Incorrect (supported)
option_judgments.justification: Evidence states separator fracture allows anode-cathode contact, establishing it as the precondition for internal short circuit.

analysis.target_cognitive_object: Identification of unsupported claims about internal short-circuit mode ranking and multi-point vs. single-point thermal risk
analysis.primary_boundary: Negation-framed stem requires identifying three unsupported statements; mechanism for Al danger is not specified in evidence
disallowed_extrapolations: Attributing Al danger to specific resistance values
Extending single-point vs. multi-point comparison beyond the annular distribution context

solver_view.prompt: A battery pack designer is evaluating two anode material candidates: traditional graphite and a silicon-containing composite. The designer knows that intercalation-induced mechanical stress is a key driver of separator damage and internal short-circuit risk. Explain, using the relationship between volume change during lithiation/delithiation and intercalation-induced stress, why silicon-containing composite anodes present a qualitatively different mechanical integrity challenge compared to graphite anodes. Also identify what separator failure consequence this stress difference implies for thermal runaway risk.

ground_truth.gold_answer: Silicon-containing composite anode materials can undergo up to 400% volume change during lithiation and delithiation, which is dramatically larger than the volume change exhibited by traditional graphite anodes. This large volumetric expansion and contraction generates substantially greater intercalation-induced mechanical stress within the electrode structure. Because separator mechanical integrity largely determines thermal runaway risk, the elevated stress from silicon anodes increases the probability of separator fracture under repeated cycling or mechanical abuse. If the separator fractures, anode and cathode materials come into direct contact, creating an internal short circuit. This internal short circuit is the proximate trigger for thermal runaway, meaning silicon-composite anodes introduce a qualitatively higher mechanical pathway to thermal runaway compared to graphite anodes.
must_include: Silicon-containing composites reach up to 400% volume change during lithiation/delithiation
This produces dramatically increased intercalation-induced stress compared to graphite
Separator mechanical integrity determines thermal runaway risk level
Separator fracture leads to anode-cathode contact and internal short circuit
Internal short circuit increases thermal runaway risk
disallowed_extrapolations: Claiming silicon anodes always cause thermal runaway under normal cycling conditions
Quantifying a specific stress threshold or fracture probability without supporting data
Asserting graphite anodes pose zero mechanical stress risk
Extending the 400% figure to all silicon composite formulations universally

analysis.target_cognitive_object: Multi-step causal chain linking anode volume change magnitude to intercalation stress, separator fracture probability, internal short circuit formation, and thermal runaway risk
analysis.primary_boundary: 400% volume change is specific to silicon-containing composites; comparison is qualitative and relative to graphite, not absolute threshold-based
intended_error_modes: scale_transfer_overclaim generalizing 400% to all Si formulations
boundary_overreach asserting graphite poses no mechanical risk

solver_view.stem: An LCO/graphite pouch cell is aged by repeated cycling at low temperature until its SOH drops from 95% to 80%. Accelerating rate calorimetry is then performed. Which of the following statements about the thermal runaway (TR) behavior are supported by the experimental evidence? Select all that apply.
options: A. The onset temperature of self-heating (T1) is significantly lower for the 80% SOH cell than for the 95% SOH cell.
B. The TR trigger time is substantially shorter for the 80% SOH cell, with values of approximately 13,313 s versus 34,295 s for the 95% SOH cell.
C. Both the pre-exponential factor and activation energy of TR side reactions are higher for the 80% SOH cell, indicating reduced thermal stability.
D. Heat generation in TR stage 3 (T2 to T3) is strongly dependent on SOH because more deposited lithium is available to react at high temperatures.
E. Self-generated heat rate increases significantly in the 30–100 °C range after low-temperature cycle aging compared to fresh cells.
F. The reduced TR trigger time at 80% SOH is primarily caused by cathode decomposition rather than anode-side lithium plating.

option_judgments.label: Incorrect
option_judgments.justification: Evidence states T1 did not change significantly across SOH 80–95%, contradicting this directional claim.

option_judgments.label: Correct
option_judgments.justification: Evidence directly reports 13,313 s vs. 34,295 s TR trigger times for 80% and 95% SOH respectively.

option_judgments.label: Correct
option_judgments.justification: Evidence states both A and Ea increase with decreasing SOH, indicating reduced thermal stability with more severe lithium plating.

option_judgments.label: Incorrect
option_judgments.justification: Evidence states stage 3 heat generation is insensitive to SOH because deposited lithium is fully consumed in stages 1 and 2.

option_judgments.label: Correct
option_judgments.justification: Evidence states self-generated heat rate increases significantly in 30–100 °C range after low-temperature cycle aging.

option_judgments.label: Incorrect
option_judgments.justification: Evidence identifies lithium plating on the anode as the most crucial safety degradation factor, not cathode decomposition.

analysis.target_cognitive_object: Distinguishing which TR behavioral changes are SOH-sensitive versus SOH-insensitive, and identifying the dominant degradation mechanism
analysis.primary_boundary: Observations are specific to LCO/graphite pouch cells aged at low temperature; stage-specific SOH sensitivity must not be generalized across all TR stages
disallowed_extrapolations: Generalizing stage-3 SOH insensitivity to all TR stages
Attributing TR acceleration to cathode rather than anode mechanisms

solver_view.stem: A researcher argues that monitoring the onset temperature of self-heating (T1) is a reliable indicator of lithium plating severity on the anode in low-temperature-aged LCO/graphite cells across SOH values of 80–95%. A second researcher proposes that T1 shifts are unreliable for this purpose but that kinetic parameters (pre-exponential factor and activation energy) are better indicators. Which assessment is best supported by the evidence?
options: A. Both researchers are correct: T1 shifts reliably with SOH, and kinetic parameters also change with SOH.
B. The first researcher is correct: T1 is a sensitive indicator because lithium-electrolyte reactions dominate heat generation at self-heating onset.
C. The second researcher is correct: T1 does not change significantly with SOH, but both kinetic parameters increase as SOH decreases, making them better indicators of plating severity.
D. Neither indicator is useful because stage 3 heat generation dominates TR behavior and is insensitive to SOH.
E. The second researcher is partially correct regarding kinetic parameters, but the claim about T1 is unsupported because T1 data across SOH 80–95% were not reported.
F. Both kinetic parameters increase with decreasing SOH, but this reflects cathode instability rather than anode lithium plating.

option_judgments.label: Incorrect
option_judgments.justification: Evidence explicitly states T1 does not change significantly across SOH 80–95%, refuting the first researcher's position.

option_judgments.label: Incorrect
option_judgments.justification: Evidence states deposited lithium contributes little to heat generation at self-heating onset, contradicting this mechanism claim.

option_judgments.label: Correct
option_judgments.justification: Evidence supports T1 insensitivity to SOH and confirms both kinetic parameters increase with decreasing SOH, indicating plating-driven instability.

option_judgments.label: Incorrect
option_judgments.justification: Stage 3 insensitivity to SOH does not negate the utility of kinetic parameters measured across all TR stages.

option_judgments.label: Incorrect
option_judgments.justification: T1 data across SOH 80–95% are explicitly reported in the evidence, making the claim of absence unsupported.

option_judgments.label: Incorrect
option_judgments.justification: Evidence attributes kinetic parameter changes to lithium plating severity on the anode, not cathode instability.

analysis.target_cognitive_object: Evaluating which measurable parameter reliably tracks lithium plating severity and why T1 is an unreliable indicator despite being a commonly used metric
analysis.primary_boundary: Findings apply to LCO/graphite cells aged at low temperature within SOH 80–95%; T1 insensitivity is specific to this chemistry and aging mode
disallowed_extrapolations: Generalizing T1 insensitivity to other chemistries or aging modes
Attributing kinetic parameter changes to cathode rather than anode

solver_view.stem: An LCO/graphite pouch cell undergoes low-temperature cycling, reducing its SOH to 85%. It is then rested at room temperature for an extended period before a thermal runaway test. Which of the following outcomes or mechanisms are consistent with the available evidence? Identify all valid statements.
options: A. Extended room-temperature rest may partially recover the cell's safety performance because deposited lithium can re-intercalate or react with the electrolyte.
B. Room-temperature rest guarantees full recovery of TR trigger time to the value of a fresh cell because all deposited lithium is reversibly re-intercalated.
C. Low-temperature aging is the most critical factor in safety degradation over the battery life cycle, making the aging history relevant even after rest.
D. Self-generated heat rate in the 30–100 °C range will remain elevated after rest because the kinetic parameters of TR side reactions are permanently altered by plating.
E. Stage 3 heat generation will become SOH-sensitive after room-temperature rest because re-intercalated lithium becomes available again at high temperatures.
F. The partial recovery mechanism involves deposited lithium either reacting with electrolyte or embedding back into the graphite anode.

option_judgments.label: Correct
option_judgments.justification: Evidence states room-temperature rest may cause partial recovery of safety performance via lithium re-intercalation or electrolyte reaction.

option_judgments.label: Incorrect
option_judgments.justification: Evidence states only partial recovery is possible; full recovery to fresh-cell TR trigger time is an unsupported universal claim.

option_judgments.label: Correct
option_judgments.justification: Evidence identifies low-temperature aging and lithium plating as the most crucial safety degradation factor over the whole life cycle.

option_judgments.label: Incorrect
option_judgments.justification: Evidence does not state that elevated heat rate in 30–100 °C range persists permanently after rest; permanence is an unsupported extrapolation.

option_judgments.label: Incorrect
option_judgments.justification: Evidence states stage 3 insensitivity arises because deposited lithium is consumed in stages 1–2; re-intercalation would reduce, not restore, stage 3 plating contribution.

option_judgments.label: Correct
option_judgments.justification: Evidence explicitly names both re-intercalation into graphite and reaction with electrolyte as the partial recovery mechanisms.

analysis.target_cognitive_object: Distinguishing partial from full recovery and identifying the mechanistic pathways for safety performance recovery after room-temperature rest
analysis.primary_boundary: Recovery is described as partial and conditional on sufficient rest or cycling; full recovery and permanent degradation claims are both unsupported
disallowed_extrapolations: Claiming full TR trigger time recovery after rest
Claiming heat rate elevation is permanent
Claiming re-intercalation restores stage 3 SOH sensitivity

solver_view.prompt: An engineer is designing a safety monitoring strategy for LCO/graphite pouch cells that have been cycled at low temperature and now span SOH values of 80–95%. She proposes using onset temperature of self-heating (T1) as the primary indicator of thermal runaway risk differentiation across this SOH range. A colleague suggests using kinetic parameters (pre-exponential factor and activation energy) instead, arguing that T1 is insensitive to SOH in this range. Explain, with mechanistic reasoning, why the colleague's recommendation is better supported by the evidence, and identify which TR stage(s) would be most informative for distinguishing risk across SOH values.

ground_truth.gold_answer: The colleague's recommendation is better supported because evidence shows T1 does not change significantly across SOH 80–95%, indicating that lithium deposited on the anode contributes little to heat generation at self-heating onset. Therefore, T1 cannot differentiate risk levels within this SOH range. In contrast, both the pre-exponential factor (lnA) and activation energy (Ea) of TR side reactions increase as SOH decreases, directly reflecting worsening lithium plating severity and reduced thermal stability. For stage differentiation, stages 1 and 2 (below T2) are most informative because deposited lithium is consumed during these stages, making their heat generation SOH-sensitive. Stage 3 (T2 to T3) is insensitive to SOH because deposited lithium is already exhausted by that point, offering no discriminatory power across this SOH range.
must_include: T1 does not change significantly across SOH 80–95%
deposited lithium contributes little to heat generation at self-heating onset
both pre-exponential factor and activation energy increase with decreasing SOH
stage 3 is insensitive to SOH because deposited lithium is consumed in stages 1 and 2
stages 1 and 2 are more informative for SOH-dependent risk differentiation
disallowed_extrapolations: Claiming T1 is universally insensitive to SOH for all chemistries or aging modes
Claiming kinetic parameters reflect cathode rather than anode degradation
Claiming stage 3 becomes SOH-sensitive under any condition within this experimental scope
Claiming full safety recovery is achievable after room-temperature rest

analysis.target_cognitive_object: Multi-step reasoning linking T1 insensitivity to its mechanistic cause, connecting kinetic parameter changes to plating severity, and mapping SOH sensitivity onto specific TR stages
analysis.primary_boundary: Findings apply to LCO/graphite pouch cells aged at low temperature within SOH 80–95%; stage-specific SOH sensitivity is a within-experiment observation not generalizable beyond this scope

solver_view.stem: A lithium-ion battery is subjected to mechanical abuse (nail penetration) at two states of charge: SOC = 0.5 and SOC = 1.0. Measured peak heat generation is approximately 8 kW at SOC = 0.5 and approximately 84 kW at SOC = 1.0. Maximum temperatures are 192°C and 665°C respectively. Which of the following statements are supported by these observations?
options: A. The roughly 10-fold increase in heat generation between SOC 0.5 and SOC 1.0 is consistent with a disproportionate rise in maximum temperature, suggesting nonlinear thermal escalation.
B. SOC influences resistance more than temperature does under mechanical abuse, because higher SOC increases the anode-cathode electrolyte potential difference, driving greater heat generation.
C. Temperature is the dominant driver of resistance change during mechanical abuse penetration, because higher temperatures accelerate all electrochemical side reactions.
D. The maximum temperature increase from 192°C to 665°C implies that doubling SOC linearly doubles the thermal runaway severity for any abuse mode.
E. At SOC = 1.0 under mechanical abuse, heat generation reaches a level (~84 kW) that cannot be explained by ohmic heating alone and requires accounting for electrochemical energy release.

option_judgments.label: Correct
option_judgments.justification: An ~10× heat generation increase producing a >3× temperature rise is consistent with nonlinear escalation supported by the reported values.

option_judgments.label: Correct
option_judgments.justification: Evidence explicitly states SOC affects resistance more than temperature under mechanical abuse via increased anode-cathode potential difference.

option_judgments.label: Incorrect
option_judgments.justification: Evidence reverses this: SOC dominates over temperature in resistance change during mechanical abuse; attributing dominance to temperature is a mechanism reversal.

option_judgments.label: Incorrect
option_judgments.justification: Evidence covers only mechanical abuse at two SOC points; extending a linear doubling rule to any abuse mode is an unsupported universal generalization.

option_judgments.label: Incorrect
option_judgments.justification: While plausible, the evidence does not decompose the 84 kW into ohmic vs. electrochemical contributions; this claim is unsupported by the provided data.

analysis.target_cognitive_object: Identifying the dominant driver of heat generation under mechanical abuse and distinguishing supported nonlinear escalation from unsupported generalizations.
analysis.primary_boundary: Findings apply specifically to mechanical abuse (penetration) at SOC 0.5 and SOC 1.0; causal decomposition of heat sources is not provided.
disallowed_extrapolations: Extending SOC-to-temperature relationship to other abuse modes
Attributing temperature as dominant resistance driver under mechanical abuse

solver_view.stem: A battery model uses an integrated equivalent-circuit approach to simulate thermal runaway under electrical abuse. The model documentation states its applicable boundary is SOC < 1 and C-rate < 1. Experimental data show: separator damage above 80°C triggers internal short circuit; SEI decomposition begins at 100°C; electrode-electrolyte reactions start above 200°C. At 3C charge rate, maximum temperature reached 209.4°C with thermal runaway duration 1200 s. Which statements correctly identify model admissibility or mechanism sequencing?
options: A. The 3C simulation result (209.4°C, 1200 s) falls outside the model's stated applicable boundary and should be treated as an extrapolation beyond validated conditions.
B. Within the model's valid boundary (C-rate < 1), separator damage at 80°C is the first threshold event, preceding SEI decomposition at 100°C and electrode-electrolyte reactions above 200°C.
C. Because the model was validated at C-rate < 1, its sequential threshold predictions (80°C → 100°C → 200°C) are directly transferable to 3C conditions without additional validation.
D. At 0.5C, the battery temperature does not reach 80°C rapidly enough to cause separator damage, so internal short circuit does not occur and thermal runaway is avoided.
E. The electrode-electrolyte reaction threshold (200°C) is reached before SEI decomposition (100°C) during electrical abuse thermal runaway, meaning SEI decomposition is not a precursor event.
F. SEI decomposition at 100°C is a necessary precursor to separator damage at 80°C because chemical degradation must precede physical damage in thermal runaway progression.

option_judgments.label: Correct
option_judgments.justification: Model boundary is explicitly stated as C-rate < 1; 3C exceeds this, making the result an out-of-boundary extrapolation.

option_judgments.label: Correct
option_judgments.justification: The three thresholds (80°C, 100°C, 200°C) are ordered by temperature, establishing a sequential mechanism chain within the model's valid regime.

option_judgments.label: Incorrect
option_judgments.justification: Transferring threshold predictions from C-rate < 1 to 3C without validation is a regime transfer error explicitly contradicted by the model's boundary statement.

option_judgments.label: Correct
option_judgments.justification: Evidence explicitly states that at 0.5C internal short circuit does not occur because temperature does not reach 80°C fast enough.

option_judgments.label: Incorrect
option_judgments.justification: Reverses the reported temperature sequence; SEI decomposition (100°C) occurs before electrode-electrolyte reactions (>200°C), not after.

option_judgments.label: Incorrect
option_judgments.justification: Evidence gives 80°C for separator damage and 100°C for SEI decomposition; separator damage threshold is lower, so SEI decomposition cannot be its precursor.

analysis.target_cognitive_object: Model admissibility judgment combined with correct sequential threshold identification under electrical abuse.
analysis.primary_boundary: Model valid only for SOC < 1 and C-rate < 1; threshold sequence applies within this boundary only.
disallowed_extrapolations: Applying validated threshold sequence directly to 3C conditions
Claiming SEI decomposition precedes separator damage

solver_view.stem: An engineer proposes using an integrated ECM-based thermal runaway model to predict battery behavior at SOC = 0.95 and C-rate = 2.5 for a fast-charging safety study. The model's documented applicable boundary is SOC < 1 and C-rate < 1. Under mechanical abuse, SOC is reported to affect resistance more than temperature does. Which of the following conclusions about this proposal are correct?
options: A. The proposed C-rate = 2.5 exceeds the model boundary; predictions at this rate are extrapolations and require additional experimental validation before use in safety decisions.
B. Because SOC = 0.95 is below the SOC < 1 boundary, the SOC condition alone is sufficient to validate the full simulation for this fast-charging scenario.
C. Both SOC and C-rate conditions must simultaneously satisfy the model boundary; since C-rate = 2.5 violates the boundary, the simulation is inadmissible regardless of SOC value.
D. The finding that SOC dominates resistance over temperature under mechanical abuse can be directly applied to predict resistance behavior under electrical abuse at C-rate = 2.5.
E. The model boundary limitation implies that thermal runaway cannot physically occur at C-rate > 1, which is why the boundary was set at C-rate < 1.

option_judgments.label: Correct
option_judgments.justification: C-rate = 2.5 exceeds the stated C-rate < 1 boundary; this makes predictions extrapolations requiring further experimental validation.

option_judgments.label: Incorrect
option_judgments.justification: Both boundary conditions (SOC and C-rate) must be satisfied simultaneously; meeting only SOC < 1 does not validate the model when C-rate is violated.

option_judgments.label: Correct
option_judgments.justification: The boundary is conjunctive (SOC < 1 AND C-rate < 1); violation of either condition renders the simulation inadmissible.

option_judgments.label: Incorrect
option_judgments.justification: The SOC-dominates-resistance finding is specific to mechanical abuse; transferring it to electrical abuse is an unsupported cross-abuse-mode generalization.

option_judgments.label: Incorrect
option_judgments.justification: The boundary reflects data availability limits, not a physical impossibility of thermal runaway above C-rate = 1; evidence shows 3C does cause thermal runaway.

analysis.target_cognitive_object: Admissibility judgment requiring simultaneous evaluation of two boundary conditions and resistance to partial-satisfaction reasoning.
analysis.primary_boundary: Model requires both SOC < 1 AND C-rate < 1; violation of either makes predictions inadmissible.
disallowed_extrapolations: Treating SOC compliance alone as sufficient for model validity
Applying mechanical-abuse resistance dominance findings to electrical abuse

solver_view.prompt: A researcher compares thermal runaway severity under mechanical abuse at SOC = 0.5 versus SOC = 1.0. Heat generation is ~8 kW at SOC = 0.5 and ~84 kW at SOC = 1.0; maximum temperatures are 192°C and 665°C respectively. The researcher claims: 'Temperature is the primary control variable for resistance during penetration abuse, so cooling the cell to reduce temperature is the most effective mitigation strategy.' Evaluate this claim using the reported evidence. Identify the correct dominant variable, explain the underlying mechanism, and state one limitation on generalizing this conclusion.

ground_truth.gold_answer: The claim is incorrect. Evidence indicates that SOC, not temperature, is the dominant driver of resistance change under mechanical abuse. The mechanism is that at higher SOC, the potential difference between anode and cathode electrolytes increases, producing substantially more heat (8 kW at SOC 0.5 vs. 84 kW at SOC 1.0) and a much higher peak temperature (192°C vs. 665°C). This suggests that SOC management—limiting charge level before mechanical stress exposure—is a more directly grounded mitigation lever than temperature control alone. A key limitation is that this SOC-dominance finding is specific to mechanical abuse (penetration); the evidence does not support extending it to electrical abuse or other abuse modes, where different mechanisms govern resistance and heat generation.
must_include: SOC is the dominant variable over temperature for resistance under mechanical abuse
Mechanism: higher SOC increases anode-cathode electrolyte potential difference, generating more heat
Heat generation values (~8 kW at SOC 0.5, ~84 kW at SOC 1.0) support the SOC dominance claim
The finding is specific to mechanical abuse and cannot be generalized to other abuse modes without further evidence
disallowed_extrapolations: Claiming temperature dominates resistance under any abuse mode based on this evidence
Extending the SOC-dominance mechanism to electrical abuse thermal runaway
Concluding that cooling is ineffective in all thermal runaway scenarios based solely on this mechanical abuse data

analysis.target_cognitive_object: Evaluating a causal claim about the dominant control variable for resistance under mechanical abuse and identifying the scope boundary of the finding.
analysis.primary_boundary: SOC-dominance over temperature for resistance is established only for mechanical abuse (penetration) within the reported SOC range of 0.5 to 1.0.

solver_view.stem: A lithium iron phosphate (LFP) battery pack is being overcharged. At the moment of inspection, the battery voltage reads 4.8 V and the surface temperature is 45°C. A thermal runaway warning system must classify the current stage and decide which single detection signal is sufficient for reliable very-early-stage warning. Which of the following statements are CORRECT given these conditions?
options: A. The battery is in the very early stage of thermal runaway because voltage is between 4.2 V and 5.2 V and temperature has not yet reached 50°C.
B. The battery has already entered the early-mid stage of thermal runaway because surface temperature is approaching 50°C.
C. Relying solely on voltage change for very-early-stage warning is unreliable in large parallel cell configurations because parallel cells suppress the fault voltage signal.
D. Gas sensor detection provides a chemistry-independent and fault-condition-independent early warning method, making it universally superior to voltage monitoring.
E. Because voltage has not yet undergone a sudden change, thermal runaway has definitely not yet started inside the battery.
F. Surface temperature exceeding 50°C is one of the two threshold conditions that would elevate the classification to the early-mid stage.

option_judgments.label: Correct
option_judgments.justification: Voltage 4.8 V falls within 4.2–5.2 V and temperature is below 50°C, satisfying the very early stage definition.

option_judgments.label: Incorrect
option_judgments.justification: Early-mid stage requires voltage >5.2 V OR temperature >50°C; 45°C and 4.8 V satisfy neither threshold.

option_judgments.label: Correct
option_judgments.justification: Evidence explicitly states parallel cells suppress fault voltage signal, undermining sole reliance on voltage for very-early warning.

option_judgments.label: Incorrect
option_judgments.justification: Gas sensor feasibility is highly dependent on fault condition and battery material; universal superiority claim is unsupported.

option_judgments.label: Incorrect
option_judgments.justification: Evidence states that by the time voltage undergoes sudden change, thermal runaway has already occurred for some time; absence of sudden change does not confirm TR has not started.

option_judgments.label: Correct
option_judgments.justification: The early-mid stage is defined by voltage >5.2 V OR temperature >50°C; surface temperature exceeding 50°C is one valid trigger.

analysis.target_cognitive_object: Stage classification using dual-threshold logic and evaluation of detection signal reliability in LFP overcharge scenarios
analysis.primary_boundary: Very early stage: 4.2–5.2 V AND temperature <50°C; early-mid stage: voltage >5.2 V OR temperature >50°C
disallowed_extrapolations: Gas sensors are universally superior regardless of chemistry or fault type
Absence of sudden voltage change confirms no internal TR has begun

solver_view.stem: Two LFP battery cells are overcharged: Cell X at a low charging rate and Cell Y at a high charging rate. Both cells start from the same initial SOC. An engineer observes that Cell Y reaches thermal runaway significantly faster than Cell X. Which of the following statements correctly explain this outcome, and which represent unsupported or incorrect reasoning? Identify ALL correct statements.
options: A. At higher charging rates, the slow temperature rise stage is shorter, causing thermal runaway to occur faster inside the battery.
B. Higher charging rate increases the rate of heat generation, which shortens the slow temperature rise stage and accelerates internal thermal runaway progression.
C. Cell Y reaches thermal runaway faster solely because higher charging rates increase the battery voltage more rapidly, bypassing the 4.2–5.2 V stage entirely.
D. The shorter slow temperature rise stage at higher charging rates is a sufficient explanation for faster thermal runaway regardless of battery chemistry or cell format.
E. Gas generation in Cell Y would be expected to occur at a lower SOC than 120% because the higher rate accelerates all electrochemical degradation thresholds proportionally.
F. The observation that higher charging rates shorten the slow temperature rise stage is consistent with the reported relationship between charging rate and thermal runaway speed.

option_judgments.label: Correct
option_judgments.justification: Evidence directly states that at higher charging rates, the slow temperature rise stage is shorter and TR occurs faster inside the battery.

option_judgments.label: Incorrect
option_judgments.justification: The heat generation mechanism link is plausible but is not stated in the evidence; adding an unsupported causal mechanism constitutes a causal overclaim.

option_judgments.label: Incorrect
option_judgments.justification: Evidence does not state that higher rates bypass the 4.2–5.2 V stage; this reverses the defined stage sequence without support.

option_judgments.label: Incorrect
option_judgments.justification: The evidence is specific to LFP overcharging; extending this to all chemistries and formats is an unsupported universal generalization.

option_judgments.label: Incorrect
option_judgments.justification: The 120% SOC gas generation threshold is a specific experimental observation; proportional scaling of all thresholds with rate is unsupported.

option_judgments.label: Correct
option_judgments.justification: This accurately restates the evidence-supported relationship between higher charging rate, shorter slow-rise stage, and faster TR without adding unsupported mechanisms.

analysis.target_cognitive_object: Distinguishing evidence-grounded causal claims from mechanistic overclaims about charging rate effects on thermal runaway progression
analysis.primary_boundary: Evidence supports the correlation between higher rate and shorter slow-rise stage for LFP overcharge only; underlying heat generation mechanism and cross-chemistry generalization are not evidenced
disallowed_extrapolations: Higher rate bypasses defined voltage stages
Proportional threshold scaling with rate
Universal applicability across all chemistries

solver_view.stem: An engineer designing a thermal runaway early-warning system for a large-format LFP battery pack considers three sensor modalities: voltage monitoring, surface temperature monitoring, and gas sensing. The pack contains a large number of cells connected in parallel. The engineer must assess the limitations of each modality for achieving very-early-stage warning. Which of the following limitations are CORRECTLY attributed to the appropriate sensor modality?
options: A. Voltage monitoring alone is unreliable for very-early warning in large parallel packs because parallel cell connections suppress the fault voltage signal.
B. Surface temperature monitoring cannot detect the very early stage because the 50°C threshold is defined as the boundary of the early-mid stage, not the very early stage.
C. Gas sensor-based early warning is universally reliable across all LFP fault scenarios once calibrated, because gas generation onset at 53°C is chemistry-specific and reproducible.
D. Voltage monitoring fails for very-early warning also because, by the time voltage undergoes sudden change, thermal runaway has already been occurring internally for some time.
E. Gas sensor reliability for early fault diagnosis is highly dependent on the specific fault condition and battery material, limiting its universal applicability.
F. Surface temperature monitoring is the most reliable single modality for very-early-stage warning because it directly measures the slow temperature rise defined in the very early stage.

option_judgments.label: Correct
option_judgments.justification: Evidence explicitly states parallel cells suppress fault voltage signal, impairing very-early voltage-based warning.

option_judgments.label: Incorrect
option_judgments.justification: The 50°C threshold marks entry into early-mid stage; temperature monitoring during the slow-rise phase below 50°C could still detect very early stage changes.

option_judgments.label: Incorrect
option_judgments.justification: Evidence states gas sensor reliability is highly dependent on fault condition and battery material; universal reliability after calibration is unsupported.

option_judgments.label: Correct
option_judgments.justification: Evidence states that by the time voltage undergoes sudden change, TR has already occurred for some time, confirming this as a second distinct limitation of voltage monitoring.

option_judgments.label: Correct
option_judgments.justification: Evidence directly states gas sensor early fault diagnosis feasibility is highly dependent on fault condition and battery material.

option_judgments.label: Incorrect
option_judgments.justification: Evidence does not rank surface temperature as the most reliable single modality; this is an unsupported comparative superiority claim.

analysis.target_cognitive_object: Correct attribution of sensor modality limitations for very-early-stage TR warning in large parallel LFP packs
analysis.primary_boundary: Voltage limitations apply specifically to large parallel configurations; gas sensor limitations apply across fault conditions and materials; no evidence ranks modalities by reliability
disallowed_extrapolations: Surface temperature is the best single modality
Gas sensors are universally reliable after calibration
50°C threshold eliminates temperature monitoring utility in very early stage

solver_view.prompt: A battery management engineer proposes using a single voltage sensor as the sole detection method for very-early-stage thermal runaway warning in a large LFP battery pack with many parallel-connected cells. The engineer argues that because the very early stage is defined by a voltage range (4.2–5.2 V), voltage monitoring is the most direct and sufficient detection method. Critically evaluate this proposal by identifying the specific limitations of voltage-based detection in this configuration and explaining why these limitations undermine the proposal's validity. Limit your answer to the evidence-supported reasoning only.

ground_truth.gold_answer: The proposal is undermined by two distinct, evidence-supported limitations. First, in a large parallel cell configuration, the fault voltage signal is suppressed by the parallel connections, making it difficult to detect the fault voltage change that defines the very early stage. Second, voltage undergoes a sudden change only after thermal runaway has already been occurring internally for some time, meaning that even when a voltage signal is eventually detected, the battery has already progressed beyond the very early stage. Together, these limitations mean that voltage monitoring alone cannot reliably achieve very-early-stage warning in large parallel LFP packs, regardless of the definitional link between voltage range and stage classification.
must_include: parallel cells suppress the fault voltage signal
by the time voltage undergoes sudden change, thermal runaway has already occurred for some time
voltage monitoring alone is insufficient for very-early warning in large parallel configurations
disallowed_extrapolations: Voltage monitoring is useless in all battery configurations
An alternative sensor modality is definitively superior without qualification
The voltage range definition itself is incorrect or unreliable

analysis.target_cognitive_object: Multi-step evaluation of voltage monitoring limitations specific to large parallel LFP packs for very-early-stage TR detection
analysis.primary_boundary: Limitations are specific to large parallel configurations and to the timing relationship between voltage change and internal TR onset; they do not universally invalidate voltage monitoring
